name: Ancient Greece
description: From the birth of democracy in Athens to Alexander's conquest of the known world — philosophy, warfare, art, and the foundations of Western civilization across 7 centuries (800-146 BCE).

facts.quizQuestion: Which Athenian statesman is credited with establishing democracy through his constitutional reforms of 508/507 BCE?
facts.explanation: Cleisthenes replaced Athens' four family-based tribes with ten new tribes organized by geography (demes), smashing the power of aristocratic clans. He expanded the Boule from 400 to 500 members and introduced sortition — random selection by lot — for government positions, along with isonomia ('equality before the law') for all male citizens.
facts.statement: Cleisthenes established Athenian democracy through his reforms of 508/507 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Cleisthenes invented the lottery as a governing tool — Athenians believed choosing leaders randomly was MORE democratic than elections, because elections favor the rich and well-connected.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ancient Athenian statesman gesturing at a map carved into a marble slab showing ten geographic regions

variants.question: What political system did Cleisthenes establish in Athens in 508/507 BCE?
Cleisthenes reorganized Athenian citizens into ten new _____ based on geography rather than family lineage.

facts.quizQuestion: How many members did the Athenian Boule (Council) have after Cleisthenes expanded it from Solon's original 400?
facts.explanation: Cleisthenes expanded the council to 500 members — exactly 50 from each of the 10 new geographic tribes, selected by lottery annually. Members had to be at least 30 years old and met in the Bouleuterion in the Agora. A rotating committee (prytaneis) of 50 served 36-day terms as a standing executive.
facts.statement: The Athenian Boule had 500 members, 50 from each of the ten tribes, selected by lottery.
facts.wowFactor: The Boule members swore an oath to 'advise according to the laws what was best for the people' — ancient politicians had a legally binding duty to give honest advice, not spin.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of rows of Athenian citizens seated in a semicircular council chamber with marble columns

variants.question: What was the name of the 500-member council that prepared legislation for the Athenian Assembly?

facts.quizQuestion: In Athenian democracy, what material did citizens use to write names during an ostracism vote to expel someone from the city for ten years?
facts.explanation: Citizens scratched names on ostraka — broken pottery pieces that were 'abundant and virtually free.' The procedure required two separate votes months apart, with a quorum of 6,000 votes needed to actually expel someone. The exiled person had ten days to leave, kept their property, and could return after exactly ten years without any stigma.
facts.statement: Athenians used broken pottery shards (ostraka) to vote for ostracism, giving the practice its name.
facts.wowFactor: Archaeologists have found thousands of these actual ostraka — including batches with the same handwriting, suggesting organized political campaigns were handing out pre-filled 'ballots' to illiterate voters 2,500 years ago.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Greek citizen scratching a name onto a pottery shard beside a large collection urn in a marble square

variants.question: The word 'ostracism' derives from the Greek word for what object used in Athenian expulsion votes?

facts.quizQuestion: How long did an Athenian ostracism exile last, after which the person could return without any loss of status or property?
facts.explanation: Ostracism exiled someone for exactly ten years. Crucially, property was not confiscated and citizens returned without stigma — it was a temporary safety valve against tyranny, not a punishment for crime. Notable victims included Themistocles, Aristides, and Cimon. The last ostracism occurred around 417 BCE.
facts.statement: Ostracism exiled a citizen from Athens for exactly ten years, after which they could return without stigma.
facts.wowFactor: Ostracism was so precisely non-punitive that the most ostracized man in Athenian history — Aristides ('The Just') — was later recalled early from exile to help fight the Persians at Plataea.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a robed Athenian man walking away from the city gates with a travel pack, the Acropolis visible behind him

variants.question: True or false: An Athenian who was ostracized lost their property and citizenship permanently.

facts.quizQuestion: What was the minimum age for Athenian male citizens to participate in the Ecclesia (the central democratic assembly)?
facts.explanation: The Ecclesia was open to all adult male citizens over age 20. It met 10 times yearly in the 5th century BCE, expanding to 40 times annually by the 4th century. However, women, enslaved people, metics (resident foreigners), and children were excluded — meaning only 10–20% of the total population could participate.
facts.statement: Athenian male citizens had to be at least 20 years old to attend the Ecclesia (Assembly).
facts.wowFactor: Athens is celebrated as the 'birthplace of democracy' — but at its most inclusive, only about 10-20% of the population could vote. Women, slaves, and foreigners together vastly outnumbered the eligible voters.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a crowd of robed Athenian men raising hands on a hilltop (the Pnyx) with the Acropolis visible in the distance

variants.question: What was the name of the Athenian citizens' assembly — the central institution of direct democracy open to all male citizens over 20?

facts.quizQuestion: Which two architects designed the Parthenon (built c. 447–432 BCE), working under the overall supervision of sculptor Phidias?
facts.explanation: Ictinus and Callicrates designed the Parthenon under Pericles' building program, with sculptor Phidias overseeing the entire sculptural program. The temple was dedicated to Athena Parthenos (Athena the Virgin). Construction began in 447 BCE as a rebuilding after Persian destruction in 480 BCE.
facts.statement: Ictinus and Callicrates were the architects of the Parthenon, built c. 447–432 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon has no perfectly straight lines — the floor curves upward at the center, the columns lean inward slightly, and the spacing is uneven. These deliberate optical corrections make it look perfectly straight to the human eye from a distance.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two architects studying stone tablets with geometric diagrams in front of a Doric temple under construction

variants.question: What structure, dedicated to Athena Parthenos, was built on the Athens Acropolis between 447 and 432 BCE?

facts.quizQuestion: What precious materials made up the colossal statue of Athena Parthenos inside the Parthenon, created by the sculptor Phidias?
facts.explanation: Phidias created the Athena Parthenos as a chryselephantine statue — gold plates over a wooden frame for drapery, with ivory carved panels for exposed skin. The statue stood approximately 12 meters tall. It is now lost, known only through ancient descriptions and small Roman copies.
facts.statement: Phidias' Athena Parthenos inside the Parthenon was chryselephantine — made of gold and ivory — approximately 12 meters tall.
facts.wowFactor: The gold used in the statue was removable — Pericles designed it that way specifically so it could be melted down to fund the military in an emergency. The statue was essentially a gold reserve disguised as a goddess.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a towering armored goddess statue gleaming gold inside a marble temple interior

variants.question: Which Athenian sculptor created both the Athena Parthenos inside the Parthenon and the Statue of Zeus at Olympia?

facts.correctAnswer: Connect Athens to its port at Piraeus
distractors: Defend the northern frontier against Macedonia
Separate the Agora from the outer city
facts.quizQuestion: What was the strategic purpose of Athens' Long Walls, built 462–458 BCE and approximately 6 kilometers long?
facts.explanation: The Long Walls connected Athens overland to its port at Piraeus, effectively making Athens 'an island within the mainland.' As long as Athens controlled the sea, it could never be starved out by a land siege. The walls were destroyed by Sparta in 404 BCE after Athens' defeat in the Peloponnesian War, and later rebuilt with Persian funding.
facts.statement: Athens' Long Walls (built 462–458 BCE) connected the city to its port at Piraeus, making it impossible to besiege by land.
facts.wowFactor: When Sparta finally won the Peloponnesian War in 404 BCE, the Spartan soldiers reportedly tore down the Long Walls 'to the music of flute girls' — celebrating the moment Athens could finally be starved into submission.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of twin parallel stone walls stretching from a city to a harbor, with ships visible in the background

variants.question: Athens' Long Walls were demolished in 404 BCE — who demanded their destruction as a condition of Athens' surrender?

facts.quizQuestion: How many rowers did a standard Athenian trireme carry, arranged in three tiers using a wooden outrigger device?
facts.explanation: A trireme carried 170 rowers in three staggered tiers (thranitai, zygitai, thalamitai), plus deck crew and 10–20 marines, totaling about 200 personnel. The three rows were staggered vertically via an outrigger called the parexeiresia. The primary weapon was a bronze ram (embolon) projecting from the bow. Maximum speed reached 9 knots.
facts.statement: An Athenian trireme had 170 rowers in three tiers, with a total crew of approximately 200.
facts.wowFactor: The trireme was so physically demanding that the rowers were not slaves — they were paid free citizens or allies, because only fit, motivated men could achieve the split-second coordination needed for ramming tactics at 9 knots.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sleek wooden warship with three rows of oars extended, a bronze ram at the prow, seen from the side

variants.question: What was the name of the ancient Greek warship with three banks of oars that formed the backbone of the Athenian navy?

facts.quizQuestion: In 483 BCE, Themistocles persuaded Athens to build a fleet of 200 triremes using wealth from what source — wealth that citizens had expected to share as a personal windfall?
facts.explanation: A major silver vein was discovered at the Laurion mines in 483 BCE. Rather than distributing the windfall among citizens (about 10 drachmas each), Themistocles convinced Athens to build 200 triremes — officially to fight the rival city of Aegina, but really preparing for the Persian threat. This fleet proved decisive at the Battle of Salamis in 480 BCE.
facts.statement: In 483 BCE, Themistocles persuaded Athens to build 200 triremes using silver from the Laurion mines rather than distributing the wealth to citizens.
facts.wowFactor: Themistocles essentially tricked Athens into building its navy — he publicly framed it as a war against Aegina (a minor rival) while secretly preparing for the Persian invasion. It was one of history's most consequential political misdirections.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of miners hauling silver ore from underground tunnels while ships are being built in a harbor above

variants.question: Which Athenian statesman persuaded his city to build a fleet of 200 triremes in 483 BCE, a decision that proved decisive at the Battle of Salamis?

facts.quizQuestion: How many kings ruled Sparta simultaneously throughout the classical period, drawn from two separate royal dynasties?
facts.explanation: Sparta had two hereditary kings ruling at the same time — from the Agiad dynasty and the Eurypontid dynasty, both claiming descent from the hero Heracles. Kings held primarily religious, judicial, and military duties, but their powers diminished as the five ephors gained influence. Kings could even be put on trial.
facts.statement: Sparta was ruled by two hereditary kings simultaneously, from the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta had two kings as a built-in check on royal power — if one king misbehaved, the other could oppose him. It was a constitutional monarchy with a literal spare king, 2,500 years before the concept of constitutional checks became fashionable.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two armored Spartan kings seated side by side on thrones in a simple stone hall

variants.question: What were the two royal dynasties whose members served simultaneously as Sparta's co-kings?

facts.quizQuestion: How many ephors were elected annually in Sparta to oversee the kings and manage civil administration?
facts.explanation: Exactly five ephors were elected yearly by the popular assembly via voice voting — the loudness of shouts determined the winner. They served one-year terms, could accompany kings on campaigns as overseers, and could even put kings on trial. Plato called the ephors 'tyrants, who ran Sparta as despots while the kings were little more than generals.'
facts.statement: Sparta had exactly five ephors, elected annually by voice vote to oversee the kings and run civil government.
facts.wowFactor: Spartan elections were conducted by pure noise — candidates walked in separately while a panel in a windowless building (so they couldn't see the crowd) judged which name produced the loudest cheer. The most-cheered candidate won.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of five stern robed officials seated behind a long stone table in a simple hall

variants.question: What was the name of the five elected Spartan officials who could oversee and even put kings on trial?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the minimum age requirement to serve as a member of the Spartan Gerousia (Council of Elders)?
facts.explanation: The Gerousia had 30 members — the two kings plus 28 elected elders (gerontes) — all serving for life. The minimum age was 60, which was exactly when Spartan citizens were no longer required to serve in the military. The Gerousia held the highest court for capital cases and could veto any action of the Assembly.
facts.statement: Members of the Spartan Gerousia had to be at least 60 years old and served for life as the highest court.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta's most powerful governing body, the Gerousia, was also elected by noise — candidates entered a room one at a time while hidden judges gauged audience reaction by cheer volume. The loudest cheer won a lifetime appointment.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of elderly Spartan men with white beards seated in a stone council chamber

variants.question: What was the name of Sparta's 30-member Council of Elders, consisting of the two kings and 28 elected members serving for life?

facts.quizQuestion: At what age were Spartan boys taken from their families to begin the agoge — the state-run military education program?
facts.explanation: Boys entered the agoge at age 7 and continued until age 21 (with full Spartiate status at 30). Training included military exercises, pain tolerance, hunting, dancing, and rhetoric. Boys faced deliberate deprivation: minimal food, sleep, and shelter. After age 12 they wore only one cloak year-round and slept on reed beds.
facts.statement: Spartan boys entered the agoge military training program at age 7, leaving their families to live in barracks.
facts.wowFactor: Spartan boys in the agoge were deliberately underfed and encouraged to steal food — but would be beaten if caught, not for stealing, but for getting caught. The lesson wasn't 'don't steal'; it was 'don't get caught.'
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of young Spartan boys in minimal clothing training with wooden spears under a harsh instructor's gaze

variants.question: What was the name of Sparta's rigorous state education program that took boys at age 7 to train them into soldiers?

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the secret Spartan unit composed of elite agoge graduates, tasked with conducting night raids to terrorize and kill helots?
facts.explanation: The Krypteia was a secretive program where exceptional young Spartans were sent into the countryside with only a knife and minimal provisions, tasked with killing helots — especially strong ones — to maintain social control. Aristotle noted that ephors 'annually declared war on the helots,' making these killings legally unpolluted acts of war rather than murder.
facts.statement: The Krypteia was a Spartan terror unit that conducted raids killing helots to maintain control over the enslaved population.
facts.wowFactor: The Krypteia was both a rite of passage AND a state terror program — young Spartans earned their warrior status by killing enslaved people in the dark. It's one of the most disturbing institutions in all of ancient history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a cloaked Spartan warrior crouching in shadow at night, dagger drawn, in dark countryside

variants.question: The Spartan Krypteia specifically targeted which group of people for systematic terror and killing?

facts.quizQuestion: At the Battle of Plataea (479 BCE), Herodotus recorded what ratio of helots to Spartan soldiers in the Greek force?
facts.explanation: Herodotus recorded 'seven helots for each of the 5,000 Spartan soldiers' at Plataea — roughly 35,000 helots supporting the Spartan army. By the 4th century BCE, estimates suggest 170,000–224,000 helots versus a rapidly declining Spartan citizen population. This demographic imbalance was the core driver of Sparta's obsessive military culture.
facts.statement: Herodotus recorded seven helots for every Spartan soldier at the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta's entire civilization was organized around one terrifying fact: the enslaved helots vastly outnumbered them. Every Spartan institution — the agoge, the Krypteia, the dual kingship, the ephors — was ultimately designed to prevent a helot uprising.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a hoplite soldier flanked by several lightly-armed attendants, mountains and a battlefield in the background

variants.question: What was the name of the enslaved agricultural population of Sparta, who vastly outnumbered their Spartan masters?

facts.quizQuestion: The Great Helot Revolt — the most significant uprising in Spartan history — was triggered in what year, following a massive earthquake?
facts.explanation: A powerful earthquake struck Sparta around 464 BCE, killing many Spartans and creating the opportunity for helots (primarily from Messenia) and perioeci to revolt. They established strongholds on Mount Ithome in what became the Third Messenian War. The revolt lasted until around 455 BCE and seriously weakened Sparta.
facts.statement: The Great Helot Revolt of 464 BCE was triggered by a major earthquake, with rebels holding out on Mount Ithome for nearly a decade.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta was so afraid of their own enslaved population that they called on Athens for military help putting down the revolt — and then humiliatingly sent the Athenian forces home when they arrived, fearing Athens might help the rebels instead.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of armed helots behind earthwork fortifications on a rocky mountain, Spartan soldiers besieging below

variants.question: The Great Helot Revolt was eventually resolved — roughly how many years did the rebels hold out on Mount Ithome?

facts.quizQuestion: When Philip II of Macedon threatened 'If I invade Laconia, I shall turn you out,' what single word did the Spartan ephors send back as their entire reply?
facts.explanation: The ephors replied with one word: 'If.' This reply implied that Sparta's resistance was not guaranteed — Philip should consider the conditional carefully. The term 'laconic' (meaning terse, concise speech) derives directly from Laconia, the region containing Sparta, because Spartans were famously brief in their communications.
facts.statement: When Philip II threatened to invade Laconia, the Spartan ephors replied with a single word: 'If.'
facts.wowFactor: Philip II never invaded Sparta. Whether 'If' was genuinely intimidating or Philip just had other priorities, the Spartans never needed to back up the bluff — and the exchange became one of the most famous one-word putdowns in human history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a messenger kneeling before five stern Spartan officials who hold up a single pottery shard with one word carved in it

variants.question: The word 'laconic' — meaning terse or blunt — derives from what Greek region that contained Sparta?

distractors: The right to vote in the Assembly
The right to serve as ephors
facts.quizQuestion: What did Spartan women receive that no other city-state in ancient Greece provided to women — making them uniquely educated among Greek women?
facts.explanation: In no other Greek city-state did women receive any formal education. Spartan women were educated in music, dancing, singing, and poetry (mousikē). They had greater legal rights than Athenian women, managed households and estates while men were at war, and were not confined to domestic seclusion. Spartan mothers were famous for their direct, assertive statements.
facts.statement: Spartan women received formal education in music, dance, and athletics — unique among all Greek city-states.
facts.wowFactor: While Athenian women were expected to stay home, rarely go out, and never speak in public, Spartan women managed entire estates, competed in athletic events, and reportedly told their sons 'come back with your shield — or on it.' Two city-states, two completely different ideas of womanhood.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Spartan women in athletic tunics competing in a running race while a crowd watches

variants.question: Spartan women managed households and estates largely because Spartan men spent most of their adult lives doing what?

acceptableAlternatives: both a market and a civic space
facts.quizQuestion: The Athenian Agora served two major simultaneous functions in democratic Athens — what were they?
facts.explanation: The Agora was both a marketplace for commerce AND the civic heart of democratic Athens — containing the Bouleuterion (council chamber), the Tholos (administrative building), the Temple of Hephaestus, and the Bema (speakers' platform). Located northwest of the Acropolis, it was where Athenian public life took place.
facts.statement: The Athenian Agora served as both a marketplace and the civic center of democratic Athens, containing the council chamber, courts, and public monuments.
facts.wowFactor: The Agora was where Socrates spent his days — wandering between market stalls and civic buildings, engaging strangers in philosophical debate. He was eventually arrested and tried in the same space where he'd been teaching for decades.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a bustling Greek marketplace with merchants, colonnaded buildings, and a temple visible on a hill in the background

variants.question: Which building in the Athenian Agora was the meeting place for the Boule (Council of 500)?

facts.quizQuestion: In the founding myth of Athens, the gods judged Athena's gift of what to be more useful than Poseidon's saltwater spring — so the city was named after her?
facts.explanation: Poseidon struck his trident into the Acropolis, producing a saltwater spring. Athena struck her spear into the ground and produced an olive tree. The gods judged Athena's gift more useful — olives provided food, oil for lamps, and wood for ships. The Erechtheion on the Acropolis was built over the very spot where this contest supposedly took place.
facts.statement: Athens was named for Athena after her gift of an olive tree defeated Poseidon's saltwater spring in a divine contest for the city's patronage.
facts.wowFactor: The Erechtheion on the Acropolis still has a sacred olive tree growing in its courtyard — planted on the exact spot where Athena's original tree supposedly stood. When the Persians burned it in 480 BCE, it reportedly sprouted back from the roots the next day.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a goddess and a sea god facing each other on a rocky hilltop — one side shows a spring, the other an olive tree, gods watching from clouds

variants.question: Which god competed against Athena for patronage of Athens, offering a saltwater spring by striking his trident into the Acropolis?

facts.quizQuestion: How long did the Peloponnesian War between Athens and Sparta last, from its start in 431 BCE to Athens' surrender in 404 BCE?
facts.explanation: The Peloponnesian War lasted 27 years (April 431 BCE to April 404 BCE). It pitted Athens and the Delian League against Sparta and the Peloponnesian League. The conflict was documented by Thucydides, an Athenian general exiled for failing to prevent Sparta's capture of Amphipolis.
facts.statement: The Peloponnesian War between Athens and Sparta lasted 27 years, from 431 to 404 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Thucydides started writing his history the moment the war began, believing it would be 'a great war, and more worth writing about than any of those which had taken place in the past.' He was right — and his account is still the most famous war history ever written.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Athenian and Spartan hoplites facing each other across a battlefield, with a city burning in the background

variants.question: Which Athenian general was exiled for 20 years after failing to defend Amphipolis, and used his time to write the definitive history of the Peloponnesian War?

facts.quizQuestion: The Plague of Athens (430–426 BCE), which killed Pericles himself, is estimated to have killed what fraction of Athens' total population?
facts.explanation: The plague killed approximately 75,000–100,000 people — roughly 25% of Athens' population of 250,000–300,000. It struck during the second year of the Peloponnesian War. Thucydides himself contracted it and survived, providing a detailed firsthand symptom description. Modern DNA analysis from 2005 of dental pulp supports typhoid fever as the likely cause.
facts.statement: The Plague of Athens (430–426 BCE) killed approximately 25% of Athens' population, including Pericles.
facts.wowFactor: Thucydides included his own near-death experience in his plague description — making it one of the oldest first-person accounts of surviving a pandemic. His description of symptoms is so detailed that modern doctors have diagnosed it from the text alone.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of sick Athenian citizens lying in a crowded street while a physician helplessly looks on

variants.question: The Plague of Athens killed Pericles in 429 BCE. What disease do modern scientists, using 2005 DNA analysis from dental pulp, believe was the most likely cause?

facts.quizQuestion: Which brilliant but controversial Athenian general was the driving force behind the Sicilian Expedition of 415 BCE — but was recalled mid-voyage to face trial and instead defected to Sparta?
facts.explanation: Alcibiades was one of three generals commanding the Athenian invasion of Sicily (with Nicias and Lamachus). Mid-voyage he was recalled to face charges related to the Herms scandal (sacred statues of Hermes had been mutilated — a bad omen). Rather than return, he defected to Sparta, advised them to fortify Decelea near Athens, and arranged for Spartan general Gylippus to reinforce Syracuse. The entire Athenian force was destroyed.
facts.statement: Alcibiades helped plan the Sicilian Expedition but defected to Sparta when recalled mid-voyage to face trial — contributing directly to Athens' catastrophic defeat.
facts.wowFactor: Alcibiades defected not once but three times: Athens → Sparta → Persia → Athens → exile. He gave each side intelligence on the others, was basically welcomed back every time, and was eventually assassinated on Spartan orders while living in Persian-controlled territory. He was the ultimate political survivor — until he wasn't.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a handsome Athenian general in a Spartan cloak, gesturing at a map of the Mediterranean

variants.question: The Sicilian Expedition (415–413 BCE) ended in total catastrophe — roughly how many Athenian survivors were imprisoned in stone quarries near Syracuse?

facts.quizQuestion: The Thirty Tyrants who ruled Athens for eight months in 404–403 BCE — executing around 1,500 people without trial — were installed by which power after Athens' defeat?
facts.explanation: After destroying 168 Athenian ships at Aegospotami and forcing Athens' surrender in 404 BCE, Spartan general Lysander installed the Thirty Tyrants — an oligarchic junta led by Critias. They ruled for eight months, executing ~1,500 people (5% of Athens' population), confiscating property, and exiling democrats. They were overthrown by exiled forces under Thrasybulus, and democracy was restored by the Amnesty of 403.
facts.statement: Sparta installed the Thirty Tyrants in Athens in 404 BCE after defeating Athens; the junta executed ~1,500 citizens in 8 months before being overthrown.
facts.wowFactor: The Thirty Tyrants killed more Athenians in 8 months than Sparta had killed in 27 years of war — and one of their targets was reportedly a plan to kill Socrates, who survived by simply refusing to carry out their orders.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of armored oligarchs seated on thrones while Athenian citizens are dragged away in chains

variants.question: What name is given to the brief oligarchic junta that ruled Athens from 404–403 BCE, installed by Sparta after Athens' defeat in the Peloponnesian War?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spartan admiral destroyed 168 Athenian ships at the Battle of Aegospotami in 405 BCE, effectively ending the Peloponnesian War?
facts.explanation: Lysander caught the Athenian fleet on shore at Aegospotami, capturing or destroying 168 ships and capturing nearly the entire Athenian naval force. This left Athens unable to import food, forcing the city to surrender in 404 BCE. Lysander then installed the Thirty Tyrants and oversaw the demolition of the Long Walls.
facts.statement: Spartan admiral Lysander destroyed 168 Athenian ships at Aegospotami in 405 BCE, ending Athens' ability to resist and forcing surrender.
facts.wowFactor: When Lysander's men tore down Athens' Long Walls 'to the music of flute girls,' the Spartan allies cheered — they believed it was the day slavery ended in Greece. They were wrong: the Thirty Tyrants showed that Athenians could oppress each other far more brutally than Sparta had.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of burning Athenian warships on a beach at night, a Spartan admiral watching from the shore

variants.question: At what naval battle in 405 BCE did Sparta decisively defeat Athens, capturing or destroying 168 ships and ending the Peloponnesian War?

facts.quizQuestion: Pericles delivered his famous Funeral Oration in approximately what year, honoring soldiers who died in the first year of the Peloponnesian War?
facts.explanation: Pericles delivered his Funeral Oration in winter 431/430 BCE, as recorded by Thucydides. It celebrated Athenian democracy, culture, and values while honoring the war dead. Pericles himself died the following year (429 BCE) from complications of the Plague of Athens. Thucydides described him as 'the first citizen of Athens.'
facts.statement: Pericles delivered his Funeral Oration in winter 431/430 BCE, a landmark speech celebrating Athenian democracy and culture.
facts.wowFactor: Pericles' Funeral Oration was probably not delivered verbatim — Thucydides admitted he reconstructed speeches to convey their 'general sense.' So what we read as Pericles' most famous words may actually be Thucydides' paraphrase of a speech he may not have witnessed himself.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a bearded orator standing on a marble platform addressing a crowd of mourning Athenians with the city visible behind

variants.question: Which Athenian leader dominated politics from 461–429 BCE, built the Parthenon, and was described by Thucydides as 'the first citizen of Athens'?

facts.quizQuestion: Metics — resident foreigners in Athens — were excluded from political participation and could not do what key economic activity that citizens could?
facts.explanation: Metics could not own land in Athens. They required a citizen patron (prostates), were excluded from all political participation, and paid special taxes. Despite these restrictions, metics were vital to the Athenian economy as craftsmen, merchants, and traders. Some prominent philosophers and artists were metics.
facts.statement: Metics (resident foreigners) in Athens could not own land, required a citizen patron, and were excluded from all political participation.
facts.wowFactor: Athens was full of wealthy, educated foreigners who contributed hugely to its culture — but couldn't vote, own land, or hold office. Some of the most important figures of the Golden Age were metics who had no legal political existence in the city they made famous.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a robed merchant at a market stall with goods, the Acropolis visible, but a faded outline showing absent civic rights

variants.question: What term did Athens use for resident foreigners who lived in the city, contributed economically, but had no political rights and could not own land?

facts.quizQuestion: Estimates suggest approximately how many enslaved people lived in Attica by 338 BCE — the people who worked the silver mines and households that funded Athenian democracy?
facts.explanation: Estimates suggest around 150,000 enslaved people in Attica by 338 BCE — completely excluded from democratic participation. The silver mines at Laurion were worked by enslaved people in grueling underground conditions, producing the wealth that funded both the Parthenon and the fleet of 200 triremes that saved Greece at Salamis. Athenian democracy's physical infrastructure rested on enslaved labor.
facts.statement: An estimated 150,000 enslaved people lived in Attica by 338 BCE — the labor force that funded Athenian democracy through silver mine work.
facts.wowFactor: The silver mines at Laurion where enslaved people worked had tunnels so narrow that only children could crawl through them. The democracy celebrated at the Parthenon was literally built on child slave labor underground.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of enslaved workers in underground tunnels hacking at silver-veined rock by torchlight

variants.question: The silver mines of Laurion near Athens were primarily worked by enslaved people. The silver revenue was repurposed in 483 BCE to build what?

facts.quizQuestion: After Ephialtes and Pericles stripped most of its political power, the ancient Athenian Areopagus council retained its role primarily as what type of court?
facts.explanation: The Areopagus was an ancient aristocratic council composed of ex-archons. It originally held great power but was progressively stripped of authority through Ephialtes' reforms (461 BCE) and Pericles' policies. It retained its role as a criminal court — especially for homicide — and as 'Guardian of the Laws' with a veto over unconstitutional actions. It met on the rocky hill northwest of the Acropolis.
facts.statement: The Areopagus, after democratic reforms stripped most of its power, retained its function primarily as a criminal court for homicide cases.
facts.wowFactor: In Aeschylus' play The Eumenides (458 BCE — just 3 years before Pericles stripped the Areopagus of most powers), the first jury trial in mythology is set at the Areopagus. Aeschylus was essentially writing political propaganda defending the old court's importance at the exact moment Pericles was abolishing it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a rocky hilltop in Athens with elderly judges seated in the open air, columns of the Acropolis visible beyond

variants.question: What Athenian institution, composed of former archons, originally held great power but was reduced by Ephialtes' 461 BCE reforms to primarily a homicide court?

facts.quizQuestion: The Parthenon was severely damaged by an explosion in what year, when a Venetian artillery shell ignited an Ottoman gunpowder magazine stored inside?
facts.explanation: In 1687, Venetian forces besieging Athens fired artillery at the Acropolis, where Ottoman forces had stored gunpowder inside the Parthenon. The explosion blew off the roof, destroyed the interior, and caused most of the current damage to the building. It had survived relatively intact for over 2,000 years before this event.
facts.statement: The Parthenon was severely damaged in 1687 when a Venetian artillery shell ignited an Ottoman gunpowder magazine stored inside the ancient temple.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon survived 2,100 years as a temple, then a church, then a mosque — only to have its worst damage caused by a single artillery shell in one night of 17th-century warfare. More of it was destroyed in 1687 than in all the previous millennia combined.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the Parthenon's roof exploding in a massive blast, the Acropolis lit by fire against a dark sky

facts.quizQuestion: After saving Greece at the Battle of Salamis, what ultimately happened to Themistocles — the hero who built the Athenian fleet?
facts.explanation: Themistocles was ostracized around 472–471 BCE due to jealousy over his fame and perceived arrogance. He fled to Argos, then to Asia Minor, and eventually entered the service of Persian King Artaxerxes I around 465 BCE — the very empire he'd defeated at Salamis. He was appointed governor of Magnesia and died there in 459 BCE.
facts.statement: Themistocles was ostracized from Athens around 472 BCE and later became a governor in the Persian Empire he had defeated at Salamis.
facts.wowFactor: The man who saved Greece from Persia ended his life as a Persian provincial governor. Artaxerxes reportedly gave him three cities to govern and a generous salary — treating the architect of Persia's greatest defeat better than Athens, the city he'd saved, ever did.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Greek general in Persian robes, looking wistfully westward from a city in Asia Minor

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spartan king reportedly replied 'Molon labe' ('Come and take them') when the Persian king Xerxes demanded the Greeks surrender their weapons at Thermopylae?
facts.explanation: Leonidas I, king of Sparta, commanded the Greek force at Thermopylae in 480 BCE. When Xerxes demanded surrender of weapons, Leonidas replied 'Molon labe' — confirmed by Plutarch's Sayings of the Spartans. Leonidas remained at the pass with 300 Spartans and 700 Thespians after the Anopaea path was revealed, fighting to the last.
facts.statement: Spartan King Leonidas I replied 'Molon labe' ('Come and take them') when Xerxes demanded Greek surrender of weapons at Thermopylae.
facts.wowFactor: 'Molon labe' became one of the most quoted military phrases in history — it appears on the emblem of the Greek 1st Army Corps and is used by military units worldwide. A two-word answer to a demand for surrender made 2,500 years ago still resonates.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a warrior-king in a red cloak and crested helmet standing in a narrow mountain pass, gesturing defiantly toward a massive army

variants.question: What does the famous Greek phrase 'Molon labe' — spoken by Leonidas at Thermopylae — mean in English?
distractors: We will fight in the shade

facts.quizQuestion: Who betrayed the Greek position at Thermopylae by revealing the Anopaea mountain path to the Persians, allowing them to outflank Leonidas?
facts.explanation: Ephialtes of Trachis revealed the Anopaea path to Xerxes, motivated by desire for monetary compensation. His name became synonymous with 'traitor' in Greek culture — 'ephialtes' later became the Greek word for nightmare. The Greeks placed a bounty on his head; he fled to Thessaly and was killed around 470 BCE. The Spartans rewarded his killer anyway.
facts.statement: Ephialtes betrayed the Greek position at Thermopylae by revealing a mountain path to the Persians, and his name became the Greek word for nightmare.
facts.wowFactor: Ephialtes' name became so universally hated that the ancient Greeks started using 'ephialtes' as their word for a nightmare — the terrifying vision that visits you in the night. His betrayal was so visceral it became embedded in the language itself.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a cloaked figure pointing a Persian soldier toward a hidden mountain path while Greek soldiers guard the pass below, unaware

facts.quizQuestion: After age 12 in the Spartan agoge, boys walked barefoot year-round and received what minimal clothing allowance annually?
facts.explanation: After age 12, agoge students received only one cloak per year — regardless of weather — and walked barefoot. They slept on beds made from river reeds. They were deliberately underfed and expected to steal food, but beaten if caught — not for stealing, but for being incompetent enough to get caught. This hardship was systematic: it built pain tolerance and resourcefulness.
facts.statement: Spartan agoge boys over age 12 received only one cloak per year, walked barefoot, and slept on reed beds.
facts.wowFactor: Spartan boys caught stealing food were beaten — but only for getting caught, not for stealing itself. The system was teaching operational security: a soldier who gets caught doing what he needs to do is a liability. Stealing food was the lesson; not getting caught was the real curriculum.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of barefoot young Spartans in thin tunics training in the snow, some stealing food from a market stall while an instructor watches

facts.quizQuestion: Pericles moved the Delian League's treasury from the sacred island of Delos to Athens in what year — enabling him to fund construction of the Parthenon with allied money?
facts.explanation: The Delian League was founded in 478 BCE as a defensive alliance of 150–330 Greek city-states under Athenian leadership against Persia. Members could contribute ships or money; most chose money, strengthening Athens while weakening allied independence. In 454 BCE, Pericles moved the treasury to Athens, essentially converting the league into an Athenian Empire and using tribute money for the Acropolis building program.
facts.statement: Pericles moved the Delian League's treasury from Delos to Athens in 454 BCE, converting allied tribute into funding for the Parthenon and other Athenian monuments.
facts.wowFactor: The Delian League's stated purpose was to fight Persia together. After Athens moved the treasury to its own city and used the money to build the Parthenon, one Athenian ally complained that 'Athens decks herself out like a harlot with expensive stones and statues using the money meant for the war.' Athens kept the money anyway.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Athenian officials loading treasury chests from a marble island onto ships bound for Athens, allied representatives watching unhappily

variants.question: The Delian League was a defensive alliance formed in 478 BCE against Persia. On what sacred island was its treasury originally kept?

facts.quizQuestion: Cyrus II (c. 600–530 BCE) founded what empire — which became the largest empire in the ancient world at that time — after conquering Media, Lydia, and Babylon?
facts.explanation: Cyrus the Great founded the Achaemenid Empire after defeating his grandfather Astyages of Media (553–550 BCE), conquering King Croesus of Lydia (546 BCE), and capturing Babylon (539 BCE). He was known for respecting conquered peoples' customs and freeing the Jewish captives from Babylon. He died in December 530 BCE battling the Massagetae nomads.
facts.statement: Cyrus the Great founded the Achaemenid Empire — the ancient world's largest — after conquering Media, Lydia, and Babylon between 550–539 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: The Cyrus Cylinder — a clay barrel inscription from 539 BCE — describes Cyrus allowing conquered peoples to keep their gods and customs. It's sometimes called the world's first human rights document, though historians note this was also very shrewd politics.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king in pointed crown standing before conquered city gates, soldiers and citizens bowing around him

variants.question: Which Persian king freed Jewish captives from Babylon in 539 BCE and allowed them to return home and rebuild their temple?

facts.quizQuestion: According to Herodotus, after Athens aided the Ionian Revolt, Persian King Darius had a servant repeat what phrase to him at every meal as a reminder to seek revenge?
facts.explanation: After Athenians helped burn Sardis during the Ionian Revolt, Darius I became obsessed with revenge. He had a servant say 'Master, remember the Athenians' (Herodotus 5.105) at each meal to keep his fury fresh. This led directly to the First Persian Invasion and the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE.
facts.statement: Darius I had a servant remind him 'Remember the Athenians' at every meal after Athens aided the Ionian Revolt that burned his satrapal capital of Sardis.
facts.wowFactor: Darius' mealtime mantra worked almost too well — he spent years preparing the largest military expedition in history to crush a tiny city-state. Athens had a population of maybe 30,000 citizens; Darius ruled millions. The disproportionate fury itself became part of Greek mythology.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king at a lavish banquet while a servant leans in to whisper a reminder, the king's face darkening with resolve

variants.question: Which Persian king organized the First Persian Invasion of Greece (492–490 BCE), sending forces that were defeated at the Battle of Marathon?

facts.quizQuestion: In 498 BCE, Athenian and Eretrian forces joined the Ionian Revolt and captured and burned what major Persian administrative city — provoking Darius' lasting anger?
facts.explanation: During the Ionian Revolt (499–493 BCE), Athens sent 20 triremes and Eretria sent 5. Together with Ionian rebels, they captured and burned Sardis — the capital of the Persian satrapy (province) of Lydia. Darius took this as a personal insult and swore vengeance. The defeat of the Ionian fleet at Lade in 494 BCE and the fall of Miletus ended the revolt.
facts.statement: Athenian and Eretrian forces helped burn Sardis (Persian satrapal capital) in 498 BCE during the Ionian Revolt, provoking Darius I's fury.
facts.wowFactor: Athens sent only 20 ships to help the Ionian Revolt — a relatively minor contribution. But that limited act of support triggered two full-scale Persian invasions, the Battle of Marathon, the Battle of Salamis, and ultimately shaped the entire course of Western civilization. Few minor military decisions have had bigger consequences.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of flames rising from a Persian palace city as Greek and Ionian soldiers retreat toward the coast

variants.question: The Ionian Revolt (499–493 BCE) ended when which major Ionian city fell to Persia, its population enslaved or deported?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Athenian general commanded the Greek forces at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE, devising the flanking tactic that destroyed the larger Persian force?
facts.explanation: Miltiades commanded at Marathon after persuading the polemarch Callimachus to vote for immediate attack. His tactical innovation: he thinned the center of the Greek line while strengthening the flanking wings. The Persians broke through the thin center but were enveloped by both wings. Persian cavalry — normally decisive — was never effectively deployed. Casualties: 192 Athenians killed vs. 6,400 Persians.
facts.statement: Miltiades commanded the Greeks at Marathon, using a tactical wing-strengthening maneuver that routed a Persian force twice their size.
facts.wowFactor: Miltiades, the hero of Marathon, died in disgrace the following year — jailed for debt after a failed personal military expedition and dying of gangrene in prison. His son Cimon had to pay off the enormous fine. Athens celebrated the victory but destroyed the victor.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an Athenian general directing hoplites to extend their battle line's wings while the Persian center charges forward

facts.quizQuestion: Herodotus documents that Pheidippides ran approximately how far BEFORE the Battle of Marathon — seeking Spartan military aid in two days?
facts.explanation: Herodotus confirms that Pheidippides (a professional long-distance runner, hemerodrome) ran approximately 240 km from Athens to Sparta in two days before the battle to request military aid. The Spartans refused to march immediately due to a religious festival. The famous 40-km 'Marathon to Athens' victory run that inspired the modern marathon race does NOT appear in Herodotus — it was invented centuries later by Lucian.
facts.statement: Herodotus records that Pheidippides ran ~240 km to Sparta before Marathon to request military aid — NOT the famous 42-km 'victory run,' which is a later invention.
facts.wowFactor: The modern Olympic marathon (42.195 km) is based on a story that Herodotus never tells. The actual documented run by Pheidippides was six times longer — Athens to Sparta and back — making the 'legendary' feat a historical footnote to the real, unbelievable achievement.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a lean messenger running through mountain passes at full sprint, with spartan mountains in the distant background

facts.quizQuestion: According to Herodotus, when a storm destroyed his first bridge cables across the Hellespont, what did Xerxes famously order done to the sea as punishment?
facts.explanation: When a storm destroyed his pontoon bridge cables across the Hellespont (modern Dardanelles), Xerxes ordered the sea flogged 300 times and fetters thrown into the water, reportedly saying the sea had 'done him wrong.' He then had the bridge engineers beheaded and built new bridges. Two pontoon bridges were eventually built: one for his army, one for his supply train.
facts.statement: Xerxes ordered the Hellespont 'whipped 300 times' and had fetters thrown into it after a storm destroyed his first pontoon bridge — then beheaded the engineers.
facts.wowFactor: Xerxes didn't just whip the sea — according to Herodotus, he had the water branded with hot irons as further punishment. It's the most unhinged response to weather in recorded history, from a man who controlled the largest empire on Earth.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Persian soldiers lashing the sea with chains while a furious king watches from shore, chains being thrown into the waves

variants.question: Xerxes' massive engineering feat to cross into Europe included digging a canal through what Greek peninsula to avoid the storm-prone cape where his earlier fleet was wrecked?

facts.quizQuestion: The Persian 'Immortals' were an elite royal military unit always kept at exactly what size — because every casualty was immediately replaced?
facts.explanation: The Immortals (Athánatoi in Greek) were a Persian elite force of exactly 10,000 soldiers — every dead, seriously wounded, or sick member was immediately replaced, so the unit was always at full strength. This replacement practice gave them the nickname 'Immortals.' They comprised primarily Persians, Medes, and Elamites and served as both imperial guard and elite fighting force.
facts.statement: The Persian Immortals were an elite force of exactly 10,000, always at full strength because casualties were immediately replaced — giving them their name.
facts.wowFactor: The Immortals couldn't break the Greek phalanx at Thermopylae by direct assault — 10,000 elite warriors were stopped by 300 Spartans in a narrow pass for two full days. The Greek formation and terrain nullified every advantage of Persian numbers and training.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Persian elite warriors in golden armor and wicker shields marching in perfect formation, with a narrow mountain pass visible ahead

variants.question: What Greek word (Athánatoi) gave the Persian elite guard their famous name, referring to the fact that their casualties were immediately replaced?

facts.quizQuestion: The 300 Spartans are famous — but how many Greeks in total fought at Thermopylae in 480 BCE, including Thespians, Thebans, and other contingents?
facts.explanation: The total Greek force at Thermopylae was approximately 7,000: 300 Spartans, 700 Thespians (who also refused to retreat and died to the last), 400 Thebans, 1,000 Phocians, and other contingents. The Thespians are often forgotten — they chose to stay and die with the Spartans when their city of Thespiae was about to be destroyed by Persia regardless.
facts.statement: About 7,000 Greeks total fought at Thermopylae, not just 300 Spartans — including 700 Thespians who also died to the last man.
facts.wowFactor: The 700 Thespians who stayed and died with the 300 Spartans are almost never mentioned in popular culture. Unlike the Spartans who were obeying military orders, the Thespians were volunteers defending a home city that was going to be burned anyway — arguably the more tragic sacrifice.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Greeks from multiple city-states — different shield emblems — standing together in a narrow rocky pass

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spartan soldier, told that Persian arrows would 'block out the sun,' replied 'Then we will fight in the shade' — recorded by Herodotus at Thermopylae?
facts.explanation: Herodotus (7.226) records that the Spartan Dienekes, told by a Persian sympathizer that enemy arrows were so numerous they would block out the sun, replied: 'Then we will fight in the shade.' Herodotus called him 'the most valiant of all the Spartans at Thermopylae.'
facts.statement: Spartan soldier Dienekes replied 'Then we will fight in the shade' when told Persian arrows would block out the sun at Thermopylae.
facts.wowFactor: 'Fight in the shade' is the ultimate expression of the Spartan character — the arrows that were meant to intimidate became a punchline. Dienekes turned a threat designed to cause fear into a promise of tactical advantage. It's been quoted in military training for 2,500 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grinning Spartan warrior gesturing dismissively at a sky filled with incoming arrows

facts.quizQuestion: At the Battle of Salamis (480 BCE), what was Themistocles' key tactical insight that gave the smaller Greek fleet an advantage over the larger Persian navy?
facts.explanation: Themistocles persuaded the Greek fleet to engage in the narrow straits between Salamis Island and the mainland. In confined waters, the Persian numerical advantage was neutralized — their larger fleet couldn't maneuver and ships collided with each other. The Greeks destroyed approximately 200–300 Persian ships while losing only ~40 vessels, 'securing Greece's survival as an independent civilization.'
facts.statement: Themistocles' winning strategy at Salamis was to fight in the narrow straits, where Persian numerical superiority became a liability as their fleet couldn't maneuver.
facts.wowFactor: Themistocles got the Persian fleet into the straits using a fake message — his servant Sicinnus told Xerxes that the Greek fleet was collapsing and would flee if the Persians moved in to block their escape. Xerxes took the bait. The battle that saved Western civilization was won by one of history's greatest deceptions.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two battle fleets clashing in a narrow strait, Persian ships tangled together while Greek triremes ram them from the side

facts.quizQuestion: At the Battle of Salamis, what did the Persian commander Artemisia I of Caria do that made Xerxes exclaim 'My men have become women; and my women, men'?
facts.explanation: Artemisia I, queen of Halicarnassus and one of Xerxes' naval commanders, was being pursued by an Athenian trireme at Salamis. To escape, she rammed and sank a friendly Calyndian ship (an ally). Xerxes, watching from his throne on the shore, saw her sink a ship and — not knowing it was an ally — assumed she'd sunk an enemy vessel, prompting his famous remark. Herodotus praised her 'decisiveness and intelligence.'
facts.statement: Artemisia I of Caria rammed a friendly ship at Salamis to escape an Athenian pursuer — Xerxes saw it and, thinking she'd sunk an enemy, praised her as his best commander.
facts.wowFactor: Artemisia was the only commander who advised Xerxes against fighting at Salamis — she predicted the Greeks would win in narrow waters. He ignored her. Then she accidentally destroyed an allied ship and was rewarded for it. Herodotus couldn't resist the irony.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a female naval commander standing at the prow of a trireme, ramming a friendly ship as an Athenian vessel bears down from behind

variants.question: Which female Persian naval commander at Salamis had warned Xerxes against engaging the Greek fleet in narrow waters — and was the only one to give him sound strategic advice?

facts.quizQuestion: At the Battle of Salamis in September 480 BCE, approximately how many Persian ships did the Greek fleet destroy while losing only about 40 of their own?
facts.explanation: The Greek fleet of 371–378 triremes destroyed approximately 200–300 Persian ships (out of an estimated 600–800 present) while losing only ~40 vessels. This lopsided outcome forced Xerxes to retreat to Asia with most of his army. The remaining Persian land force under Mardonius was defeated the following year at Plataea (479 BCE), ending the invasion.
facts.statement: At Salamis, the Greeks destroyed 200–300 Persian ships while losing only ~40 of their own, forcing Xerxes to retreat from Greece.
facts.wowFactor: Xerxes watched his navy get destroyed from a golden throne set up on the shore. He could see every ship that sank. Herodotus says he tore his robes when he understood what was happening — the most powerful man on Earth watching his invasion fail in real time.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king watching from a hilltop throne as his fleet burns in the straits below, Greek ships victorious around them

variants.question: After his defeat at Salamis in 480 BCE, where did Xerxes set up his throne to watch the naval battle — later retreating from Greece with most of his army?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spartan regent commanded the combined Greek forces at the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE — the land victory that ended the Persian invasion of Greece?
facts.explanation: Pausanias, regent of Sparta (serving for his underage cousin), commanded the Greek forces at Plataea. Under his leadership the Greek army (~75,000–85,000) defeated the Persian force under Mardonius, who was killed in battle. Combined with the simultaneous Battle of Mycale, Plataea 'decisively ended the invasion.' Pausanias was later accused of conspiring with Xerxes and starved to death by being walled into a temple.
facts.statement: Spartan regent Pausanias commanded the Greek victory at Plataea in 479 BCE, the decisive land battle that ended the Persian invasion.
facts.wowFactor: Pausanias — the man who saved Greece at Plataea — was later starved to death by being sealed alive in a Spartan temple after being accused of conspiring with the very Persians he'd defeated. His own mother reportedly helped seal the entrance.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Spartan general on horseback directing the Greek phalanx against Persian cavalry, a Persian commander falling from his horse

variants.correctAnswer: The Persian left wing routed and the army collapsed

facts.quizQuestion: Which Persian general, left by Xerxes to command Persian forces in Greece after the defeat at Salamis, was killed at the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE — causing the Persian army to rout?
facts.explanation: After Salamis, Xerxes retreated to Asia and left Mardonius in command of the Persian land army in Greece. At Plataea (479 BCE), Mardonius was killed in battle — his death caused the Persian left wing to rout immediately, collapsing the entire Persian position. Herodotus recorded that Mardonius had been the strongest advocate for the Greek invasion.
facts.statement: Persian general Mardonius, left in command after Salamis, was killed at Plataea in 479 BCE — his death instantly collapsed the Persian army.
facts.wowFactor: The Battle of Plataea — the decisive land battle that ended Persia's invasion — was essentially decided by the death of one man. When Mardonius fell, the entire Persian formation collapsed in minutes. Ancient battles often turned on such singular moments.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Persian troops breaking and fleeing after their general falls from his horse, Greek spearmen advancing through the gap

variants.correctAnswer: Ended the Second Persian Invasion of Greece entirely
distractors: Ended the First Persian Invasion begun by Darius

facts.quizQuestion: Roman orator Cicero gave Herodotus of Halicarnassus what title, recognizing his Histories as the earliest surviving Greek prose account of the Persian Wars?
facts.explanation: Herodotus (c. 484–425 BCE) wrote the Histories — a comprehensive account of the Greco-Persian Wars. Cicero called him 'the Father of History.' His work is 'the earliest Greek prose to have survived intact.' He was born in Halicarnassus (modern Bodrum, Turkey) — which was under Persian control — making his detailed account of the wars uniquely positioned between both cultures.
facts.statement: Cicero gave Herodotus of Halicarnassus the title 'Father of History' for his Histories — the oldest surviving Greek prose work, covering the Greco-Persian Wars.
facts.wowFactor: Herodotus was born in a Persian-controlled city, traveled extensively through the Persian Empire, and interviewed Persian sources alongside Greeks. The first historian wasn't writing from one side — he was writing from both, which is why his account is still remarkable to read.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a scholar writing on a scroll by lamplight, maps and inscribed tablets covering the walls around him

variants.question: What is the name of Herodotus' major work — the first great work of Western historical writing, covering the rise of Persia and the Greco-Persian Wars?

facts.quizQuestion: Darius I died in October of what year while preparing a second, larger invasion of Greece — passing the task of revenge against Athens to his son Xerxes?
facts.explanation: Darius I (reigned 522–486 BCE) died from illness in October 486 BCE at approximately age 64, before completing his preparations for a second invasion of Greece. He was entombed at Naqsh-e Rostam. His son Xerxes I inherited both the throne and the mission of punishing Greece, eventually launching the massive Second Persian Invasion in 480 BCE.
facts.statement: Darius I died in October 486 BCE while preparing a second invasion of Greece, leaving the task of revenge to his son Xerxes I.
facts.wowFactor: Marathon delayed the Persian revenge against Athens by ten years — Darius died before he could launch his follow-up invasion. Xerxes spent years building what became the largest invasion force in ancient history to complete his father's unfinished business.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king lying on his deathbed surrounded by courtiers, maps and military plans visible on a table nearby

variants.question: After defeating the Greek force at Marathon in 490 BCE and preparing a second invasion, Darius I died before executing it. Who completed his mission?

facts.quizQuestion: Xerxes I was assassinated in August 465 BCE by whom — the commander of his own royal bodyguard?
facts.explanation: Xerxes I was assassinated in August 465 BCE by Artabanus, commander of the royal bodyguard (hazarapatish). He had reigned from October 486 BCE to August 465 BCE. His son Artaxerxes I succeeded him. The assassination ended the reign of the king who had invaded Greece, burned Athens, and lost at Salamis and Plataea.
facts.statement: Xerxes I was assassinated in 465 BCE by Artabanus, commander of his own royal bodyguard — ending the reign of the king who invaded Greece.
facts.wowFactor: The man who burned the Acropolis of Athens, whipped the Hellespont, and brought the largest army in the ancient world into Europe was murdered in his own bedroom by his top bodyguard. The greatest invasion in history ended not with a battle, but with a palace intrigue.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king surprised in his royal chambers, his crown fallen, a court official standing nearby

variants.question: After Salamis (480 BCE) and Plataea (479 BCE), Xerxes left Mardonius in charge of the Persian army in Greece and retreated to Asia. Why did he rush the retreat, according to Herodotus?
variants.correctAnswer: He feared the Greeks would destroy his pontoon bridges across the Hellespont, trapping him in Europe
distractors: He received news of a revolt in Babylon
He was wounded at Salamis and needed medical treatment
Persian law required kings to return after a year abroad
He ran out of supplies after the loss at Salamis

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Battle of Marathon take place — the decisive Athenian victory that stopped the First Persian Invasion?
facts.explanation: The Battle of Marathon was fought on September 12, 490 BCE (6 Boedromion in the Attic calendar). Greek forces under Miltiades (9,000 Athenians + 1,000 Plataeans) defeated a Persian force of ~25,000 infantry. Casualties: 192 Athenians vs. 6,400 Persians counted on the battlefield. The victory was so overwhelming that it became a defining moment in Greek identity.
facts.statement: The Battle of Marathon was fought in 490 BCE — the Athenian victory that stopped Persia's First Invasion.
facts.wowFactor: The 192 Athenian dead at Marathon were buried in a communal burial mound (the Soros) on the battlefield itself — the only Athenians ever given this honor. The mound is still there today and still 10 meters tall after 2,500 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Athenian hoplites with bronze shields advancing in a phalanx across a coastal plain, Persian ships visible on the beach behind the enemy

variants.question: The modern Olympic marathon distance of ~42 km was inspired by a legend associated with which ancient battle?

facts.quizQuestion: Which play by Aeschylus (performed 472 BCE) is based on the Battle of Salamis — and is unusually told entirely from the Persian perspective, making it the oldest surviving history play?
facts.explanation: Aeschylus' The Persians (472 BCE) dramatizes the Persian defeat at Salamis from the Persian court's perspective — Xerxes returning in rags while the queen and ghost of Darius lament the disaster. Aeschylus had personally fought at both Marathon (490 BCE) and Salamis (480 BCE). It's the oldest surviving complete Greek tragedy and the only one based on a contemporary historical event rather than myth.
facts.statement: Aeschylus' The Persians (472 BCE) retells the Battle of Salamis from the Persian perspective — written by a playwright who personally fought in the battle.
facts.wowFactor: Aeschylus fought at Salamis, then wrote a play about it from the enemy's point of view just 8 years later. His epitaph, reportedly written by himself, mentions only that he 'fought at Marathon' — not that he was the founder of tragedy, wrote 90 plays, or won 13 dramatic competitions. He considered being a soldier more important than being a playwright.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian queen in grief on stage before an audience, a ghost rising from the ground before her, marble theater columns around them

variants.question: Aeschylus, playwright and 'Father of Tragedy,' personally fought in which two Persian War battles?

distractors: Died heroically at the Battle of Plataea
Was executed by democratic vote after a trial
Was exiled to Persia like Themistocles

facts.quizQuestion: What happened to Miltiades — the hero of Marathon — within a year of his great victory, after a failed personal military expedition?
facts.explanation: In 489 BCE, Miltiades led 70 ships against pro-Persian Aegean islands — reportedly a personal vendetta against Paros. The expedition failed, and he suffered a serious leg wound. He was charged with treason in Athens; his death sentence was commuted to a 50-talent fine. He died in prison from gangrene before his son Cimon could pay the debt.
facts.statement: Miltiades, hero of Marathon, was charged with treason after a failed 489 BCE expedition and died in prison from gangrene before his enormous fine could be paid.
facts.wowFactor: Athens saved itself from Persia at Marathon, then imprisoned its savior within a year. Miltiades died in a jail cell of an infected leg wound. His son Cimon — who later became a great Athenian general — inherited the debt and had to pay it off over years. Athens was not a forgiving city.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a wounded general in chains on a stone prison floor, one leg bandaged, the walls of Athens visible through a small window

variants.question: Miltiades died with an enormous 50-talent fine unpaid. Which family member eventually paid off this debt?

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the three-day Battle of Thermopylae take place, when 300 Spartans and allied Greeks held the pass against Xerxes' invasion force?
facts.explanation: The Battle of Thermopylae was fought in 480 BCE for three days. Greek forces under Leonidas I held the narrow pass, blocking the entire Persian advance, before being outflanked via the Anopaea mountain path revealed by the traitor Ephialtes. The same year saw the Battle of Salamis (September 480 BCE) — the naval counterpart that saved Greece.
facts.statement: The Battle of Thermopylae was fought in 480 BCE — the same year Xerxes invaded Greece and Athens was burned.
facts.wowFactor: 480 BCE was the most dramatic single year in Greek history: Thermopylae, the Persian sack of Athens (which Athenians abandoned), and the Battle of Salamis — all within a few months. Greece lost its most famous city but saved its civilization.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of armored hoplites in a tight mountain pass formation with rocky cliffs above and the sea below

variants.question: In 480 BCE, after Thermopylae and before Salamis, Xerxes achieved a shocking symbolic victory. What did Persian forces do to Athens?
variants.correctAnswer: They burned and sacked the city, including the Acropolis
distractors: They besieged it for six months but couldn't take it
They looted the treasury but left the buildings standing
They installed a Persian governor without destroying anything
They bypassed it completely to attack Sparta instead

facts.quizQuestion: During what six-year period did the Ionian Revolt — the direct cause of Darius' Persian invasions of Greece — take place?
facts.explanation: The Ionian Revolt lasted from 499 to 493 BCE (six years), across Asia Minor and Cyprus. It was triggered by dissatisfaction with Persian-appointed tyrants and the failed expedition to conquer Naxos (499 BCE). The revolt ended with the defeat of the Ionian fleet at Lade (494 BCE) after Samian defection, and the fall of Miletus.
facts.statement: The Ionian Revolt lasted 499–493 BCE — six years of Greek cities in Asia Minor rebelling against Persian rule, directly triggering Darius' invasions of Greece.
facts.wowFactor: The Ionian Revolt failed completely — every rebel city was retaken, Miletus was destroyed, and Persian control was restored. But the revolt didn't fail: it triggered Persia to invade mainland Greece, which then triggered Salamis, Plataea, and the entire cultural explosion of 5th-century Athens. A failed rebellion accidentally created Western civilization.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a map showing Greek cities along the coast of Asia Minor with some highlighted in flames and others in resistance

variants.question: The Ionian Revolt was initiated by Aristagoras of Miletus after what failed Persian military expedition provided his personal motivation to rebel?
variants.correctAnswer: A failed expedition to conquer Naxos (499 BCE)
distractors: A failed attempt to conquer Cyprus

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately when was Themistocles ostracized from Athens — despite having built the navy that saved Greece at Salamis?
facts.explanation: Themistocles was ostracized around 472–471 BCE, roughly eight years after the Battle of Salamis that he had orchestrated. The reasons were political jealousy, perceived arrogance, and suspicion of pro-Spartan sympathies in the climate following the Persian Wars. He fled to Argos, then eventually entered Persian service — becoming governor of Magnesia under Artaxerxes I.
facts.statement: Themistocles was ostracized ~472 BCE — just 8 years after saving Greece at Salamis — due to political jealousy and suspicion.
facts.wowFactor: Athens ostracized the man who literally saved Greek civilization. Within a decade of Salamis, the hero who persuaded them to build a fleet, who tricked Xerxes into the narrow straits, who designed the entire winning strategy — was expelled from the city he saved. Athens was famously ungrateful.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of pottery shards being thrown into an urn in a packed Athenian square, one shard showing a man's name scratched in Greek letters

variants.question: After being ostracized, Themistocles eventually found refuge in the Persian Empire — which king welcomed him and made him a provincial governor?

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Battle of Plataea fought — the decisive Greek land victory that, combined with the simultaneous Battle of Mycale, ended the Persian invasion?
facts.explanation: The Battle of Plataea was fought on August 27 or 28, 479 BCE, in Boeotia, Greece. Greek forces (~75,000–85,000) under Spartan regent Pausanias defeated the Persian army under Mardonius (who was killed). The simultaneous Battle of Mycale (also 479 BCE) destroyed the remainder of the Persian fleet and ended the Second Persian Invasion.
facts.statement: The Battle of Plataea (479 BCE) — the decisive Greek land victory — ended the Persian invasion when Mardonius was killed and his army routed.
facts.wowFactor: Plataea and Mycale happened on the same day in August 479 BCE, in completely different parts of the Greek world. The Greeks on land and sea defeated Persia simultaneously — as if the whole war ended at once in two places at the same moment.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Greek hoplites in tight formation advancing over a Persian dead, with mountains in the background and distant smoke from another battle

variants.question: The Battle of Plataea (479 BCE) and Battle of Mycale (479 BCE) together ended the Second Persian Invasion. What year was the invasion launched?

facts.correctAnswer: Walled into a temple and starved to death
acceptableAlternatives: sealed in a temple and starved
distractors: Executed by democratic vote in Sparta
