facts.quizQuestion: The Spartan regent Pausanias, who commanded the Greek victory at Plataea, met what grim end after being accused of conspiring with Xerxes?
facts.explanation: Pausanias was accused of treasonous correspondence with Xerxes around 478 BCE. When he sought sanctuary in the Temple of Athena of the Brazen House, Spartans bricked up the entrance. He was starved to death inside the temple, still technically 'protected' by the sanctuary. According to ancient sources, his own mother reportedly helped place the first stone to seal the entrance.
facts.statement: Pausanias, hero of Plataea, was accused of conspiring with Xerxes and was bricked into a temple by Spartans — starved to death while his own mother reportedly helped seal the entrance.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta couldn't execute Pausanias in a temple without committing sacrilege — so they walled him in instead, letting him starve while technically 'respecting' the sanctuary. When he was near death, they dragged him out so he wouldn't pollute the sacred space with a corpse. Ancient legal creativity at its darkest.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Spartan soldiers laying stones to seal a temple entrance while a figure is barely visible through the shrinking gap inside

variants.question: Pausanias sought sanctuary in a Spartan temple when accused of treason. What specific sanctuary did he flee to?
variants.correctAnswer: The Temple of Athena of the Brazen House
distractors: The Temple of Zeus at Olympia
The Temple of Apollo at Delphi
The Parthenon on the Athenian Acropolis

facts.quizQuestion: What was the composition of the Greek force at Marathon in 490 BCE — notably fought without Spartan assistance despite Pheidippides' urgent request?
facts.explanation: The Greek force at Marathon comprised approximately 9,000 Athenians and 1,000 Plataeans — the Plataeans were the only allied contingent. The Spartans declined to march until after a religious festival (the Carneia). This ~10,000-man Greek force defeated a Persian army estimated at ~25,000 infantry plus cavalry, suffering 192 Athenian and 11 Plataean dead against ~6,400 Persian casualties.
facts.statement: The Greeks at Marathon numbered ~9,000 Athenians and 1,000 Plataeans — no Spartans, despite Pheidippides' urgent 240-km run to request their help.
facts.wowFactor: The Plataeans sent every man they had to Marathon — their entire military force, leaving their own city undefended. They were motivated by gratitude: Athens had previously defended Plataea against Thebes. It was a genuine act of military solidarity, and Athenians never forgot it — Plataeans fought beside them for generations.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two contingents of hoplites — Athenian owls and Plataean emblems on their shields — forming a combined battle line

variants.question: Why did Sparta fail to send forces to help Athens at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE, despite Pheidippides running 240 km to request aid?
variants.correctAnswer: They were observing a religious festival (the Carneia) and could not march until the full moon
distractors: They refused to help because Athens was a rival
They were at war with Corinth at the time
They believed Athens would lose and didn't want to be involved
They arrived after the battle was already over

facts.quizQuestion: When Cyrus the Great conquered Lydia in 546 BCE, which legendarily wealthy king did he defeat — a ruler who had famously consulted the Delphic Oracle before the campaign?
facts.explanation: Cyrus defeated Croesus of Lydia in 546 BCE, adding Anatolia to the Achaemenid Empire. Croesus was proverbially wealthy ('rich as Croesus') and had consulted the Delphic Oracle, which told him 'if you cross the Halys River, a great empire will fall' — he assumed it meant Persia's. The great empire that fell was his own. The conquest of Lydia brought Persian rule to the eastern Greek (Ionian) coastal cities.
facts.statement: Cyrus the Great defeated King Croesus of Lydia in 546 BCE, bringing the wealthy kingdom and nearby Greek Ionian cities under Persian rule.
facts.wowFactor: The Delphic Oracle's prediction to Croesus is the most famous example of deliberate ambiguity in history. 'A great empire will fall' was technically true — just not the empire Croesus expected. The Oracle was never wrong, only misunderstood. It's been making philosophers argue for 2,500 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Persian king accepting a golden crown from a defeated ruler on a grand staircase, Anatolian mountains in the background

variants.question: Cyrus' conquest of Lydia in 546 BCE placed the Greek Ionian city-states of Asia Minor under Persian rule — which eventually caused what chain of events?
variants.correctAnswer: Ionian resentment led to the Ionian Revolt (499 BCE) and then the Persian Wars
distractors: Greek cities voluntarily joined the Persian Empire
Persia and Greece immediately went to war
Sparta liberated the Ionian cities within a decade
Athens allied with Lydia against Persia

facts.quizQuestion: How large was the Greek fleet at the Battle of Salamis in 480 BCE, of which Athens contributed 180 ships?
facts.explanation: The Greek fleet at Salamis comprised 371–378 triremes, with Athens contributing 180 ships — the largest single contingent, built with Laurion silver on Themistocles' initiative in 483 BCE. The Persian fleet is estimated at 600–800 vessels actually present (ancient sources claimed 1,207). Despite the numerical disadvantage, the Greeks destroyed 200–300 Persian ships while losing only ~40.
facts.statement: The Greek fleet at Salamis numbered 371–378 triremes; Athens contributed 180 ships — the single largest contingent — against an estimated 600–800 Persian vessels.
facts.wowFactor: Athens contributed nearly half the Greek fleet at Salamis. Without Themistocles' earlier decision to use Laurion silver for ships instead of personal distributions, Athens might have sent 50 ships instead of 180 — and the Greeks would have been badly outnumbered even in the narrow straits.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of hundreds of Greek triremes arrayed in the narrow Salamis straits, different city-state emblems on each prow

variants.question: Athens contributed 180 ships to the Battle of Salamis — the fleet built in 483 BCE from silver mined where?
variants.correctAnswer: The Laurion silver mines in Attica
distractors: Trade revenues from the Black Sea
A gift from the Delphic Oracle's treasury

facts.quizQuestion: Which two Persian commanders did Darius I send to lead the First Persian Invasion that ended at Marathon in 490 BCE?
facts.explanation: Darius sent Admiral Datis and his nephew Artaphernes (the Younger) to command the 490 BCE expedition against Athens and Eretria. They captured Eretria first (enslaving its population as Darius had promised), then advanced to the plain of Marathon, where they were defeated by Miltiades' Athenian-Plataean force.
facts.statement: Darius sent Admiral Datis and his nephew Artaphernes to command the expedition that was defeated at Marathon in 490 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Before Marathon, Datis captured Eretria — the other city that had helped burn Sardis — and enslaved its entire population as promised. Athens was next. The Athenians knew exactly what fate awaited them if Miltiades didn't win.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two Persian commanders on warship decks directing a fleet toward a coastline, with soldiers visible in the holds

variants.question: Before reaching Marathon, the Persian force under Datis and Artaphernes captured and enslaved the population of which Greek city that had also aided the Ionian Revolt?

facts.quizQuestion: Xerxes ordered a canal dug through what Greek peninsula before his 480 BCE invasion — avoiding the storm-prone cape where his father's fleet was wrecked in 492 BCE?
facts.explanation: Xerxes had a canal dug through the Mount Athos peninsula in northern Greece before his 480 BCE invasion. In 492 BCE, Darius' earlier invasion fleet had been devastated by a storm off Cape Athos, losing ~300 ships. Xerxes was taking no chances — digging a 2-km canal to allow his fleet to bypass the dangerous cape entirely. Ancient writers were amazed by the engineering feat.
facts.statement: Xerxes had a canal dug through the Mount Athos peninsula to avoid the storm-prone cape where Darius' 492 BCE fleet lost 300 ships.
facts.wowFactor: The Xerxes Canal through Mount Athos was so massive that ancient writers suspected Xerxes did it out of arrogance — to show he could cut through a mountain — rather than pure military necessity. But modern archaeologists have actually found the canal. It was real, and it worked.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of thousands of Persian workers digging a channel through a rocky peninsula, ships waiting to pass through in the distance

variants.question: In addition to the Athos canal, Xerxes built two pontoon bridges across what waterway to move his army from Asia into Europe?

distractors: A massive storm destroyed Ionian ships
facts.quizQuestion: The Ionian fleet was defeated at the Battle of Lade (494 BCE), effectively ending the Ionian Revolt, primarily because of what event during the battle?
facts.explanation: At the Battle of Lade (494 BCE) off Miletus, the Ionian fleet outnumbered the Persians but was defeated primarily because the Samian contingent defected during the battle — withdrawing their ships and prompting a chain collapse of the Ionian line. Miletus fell shortly after; its population was enslaved or deported, ending the Ionian Revolt.
facts.statement: The Ionian fleet lost the Battle of Lade (494 BCE) when the Samians defected mid-battle, collapsing the Ionian line and leading to the fall of Miletus.
facts.wowFactor: The Samians who defected at Lade hadn't been bribed — they had been secretly negotiating with Persia for favorable terms. Their commander Aiacas was a former Samian tyrant that Persia had deposed; the deal was his reinstatement. One man's political rehabilitation cost the entire Ionian Revolt.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of naval battle in chaos — some ships pulling back while others continue fighting, a city on the shore watching the outcome

variants.question: After the Battle of Lade and the fall of Miletus in 494 BCE, what happened to Miletus' population?
variants.correctAnswer: They were enslaved or deported by the Persians
distractors: They fled to Athens and were given citizenship
They negotiated a peace and remained in the city
Most escaped by sea to Sicily

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of Themistocles' servant who was sent as a secret messenger to Xerxes with a false report that the Greek fleet was disintegrating — luring the Persians into the narrows at Salamis?
facts.explanation: Themistocles sent his servant Sicinnus to Xerxes with a fabricated message: the Greek fleet was in chaos and about to flee; if Xerxes moved in immediately to block the escape, he could destroy them all. Xerxes ordered his fleet to advance into the narrow straits. This deception is described in Herodotus (8.75) and is considered one of the greatest strategic deceptions in military history.
facts.statement: Themistocles sent his servant Sicinnus to Xerxes with a false message that lured the Persian fleet into the narrow Salamis straits — enabling the Greek victory.
facts.wowFactor: Sicinnus was Themistocles' children's tutor. The man whose message saved Western civilization was a household teacher chosen for the mission because he spoke Persian and could pass as a legitimate messenger. After Salamis, Themistocles rewarded him with citizenship in a Greek city and considerable wealth.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a lone figure in plain clothes approaching a massive Persian warship, torchlight revealing Persian guards who let him aboard

variants.question: Themistocles' deception at Salamis — sending a false message to Xerxes — is described by which ancient historian in his account of the battle?

They lacked sufficient food for the march
facts.quizQuestion: What prevented Sparta from marching to help Athens at Marathon in 490 BCE — the same reason Spartan forces also arrived too late for Thermopylae in 480 BCE?
facts.explanation: At Marathon (490 BCE), the Spartans were observing the Carneia — a sacred festival during which Spartan law forbade military campaigns until the full moon. They sent 2,000 men after the festival ended, arriving after the battle was over. A similar religious constraint (possibly Hyacinthia or Carneia) delayed Spartan response before Thermopylae.
facts.statement: Sparta couldn't help Athens at Marathon in 490 BCE because of the Carneia — a sacred festival during which Spartan law prohibited military campaigns.
facts.wowFactor: Sparta's religious calendar potentially cost Athens at Marathon — and possibly at Thermopylae. The Spartans were genuinely constrained, not making excuses: the same law that made them arrive late also meant the 300 who went to Thermopylae did so in defiance of religious law, under Leonidas' personal initiative.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Spartan soldiers at a religious ceremony with lyre-playing priests, while a distant messenger arrives with urgent news they must refuse

variants.question: The 2,000 Spartan soldiers arrived at Marathon after the battle was over. What did they do when they found the Persian dead on the battlefield?
variants.correctAnswer: They walked through the battlefield and praised the Athenian victory before marching home
distractors: They helped pursue the retreating Persians to their ships
They were ashamed and left without acknowledging Athens
They stayed to help garrison Marathon against a second landing
They looted the Persian camp and split the spoils with Athens

facts.quizQuestion: What was Pericles' official elected role in Athens — the position he held repeatedly to dominate Athenian politics from 461 to 429 BCE?
facts.explanation: Pericles was never a king or dictator — his official title was strategos (general/military commander), an elected position. He was voted into this role year after year, and through it he directed both Athenian military policy and the construction of the Parthenon. Thucydides called him 'the first citizen of Athens' but that was a description, not a title.
facts.statement: Pericles' official role in Athens was strategos (elected general), not a hereditary or dictatorial title.
facts.wowFactor: The man who built the Parthenon and shaped the Golden Age of Athens never had a formal title beyond 'elected general' — he achieved unmatched influence through persuasion and democratic reelection alone.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a helmeted Athenian general standing at a speaker's podium before a packed assembly on the Pnyx hill

variants.question: True or False: Pericles was a hereditary king who ruled Athens from 461 to 429 BCE.
Pericles dominated Athenian politics by repeatedly winning election as _____, a military commander role.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Pericles deliver his famous Funeral Oration — the speech honoring soldiers killed in the first year of the Peloponnesian War that became synonymous with Athenian democratic ideals?
facts.explanation: Pericles delivered the Funeral Oration in winter 431/430 BCE at the public ceremony for those who died in the first year of the Peloponnesian War. As recorded by Thucydides, the speech celebrated Athens as a model for all of Greece and articulated democratic values. It became one of the most influential political speeches in Western history.
facts.statement: Pericles delivered his Funeral Oration in winter 431/430 BCE to honor soldiers fallen in the first year of the Peloponnesian War.
facts.wowFactor: Pericles gave his Funeral Oration knowing he was likely signing his own death warrant — the war he couldn't avoid was already creating the crowded conditions in Athens that would spread the plague killing him a year later.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a robed statesman gesturing over rows of torches at a solemn outdoor ceremony at dusk

variants.question: What occasion did Pericles' famous Funeral Oration mark?
variants.correctAnswer: Honoring soldiers who died in the first year of the Peloponnesian War
distractors: Victory over the Persians at Salamis
The founding of the Delian League

facts.quizQuestion: Which two architects were responsible for designing the Parthenon, whose construction began in 447 BCE under Pericles?
facts.explanation: The Parthenon was designed by Iktinos (also spelled Ictinus) and Callicrates, working under the artistic direction of sculptor Phidias. The building took 15 years (447–432 BCE) and housed a massive gold-and-ivory statue of Athena by Phidias. It stands today as the finest surviving example of Classical Greek architecture.
facts.statement: The Parthenon was designed by architects Iktinos and Callicrates, with Phidias overseeing the sculpture program.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon was built with deliberately curved floors and slightly tapered columns — subtle optical corrections to prevent the building from looking warped to the human eye. No straight lines were used.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two architects studying a large papyrus blueprint with the Acropolis rocky hill visible behind them

variants.question: Who was the artistic director who supervised the entire sculptural program of the Parthenon?

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did construction of the Parthenon begin under Pericles' building program on the Acropolis?
facts.explanation: Construction on the Parthenon started in 447 BCE and was completed in 432 BCE — a 15-year project. It was funded by diverting tribute money from the Delian League treasury (moved from Delos to Athens in 454 BCE). The gold-and-ivory statue of Athena Parthenos inside was dedicated in 438 BCE.
facts.statement: The Parthenon was built from 447 to 432 BCE on the Athens Acropolis.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon survived intact for over 2,000 years until 1687, when a Venetian artillery shell hit the Ottomans' gunpowder magazine stored inside — destroying the building's center in a single explosion.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of workers in ancient dress hauling marble blocks up the Acropolis slope with ropes and ramps

variants.question: The Parthenon's construction was funded by diverting tribute from the _____ treasury, which Pericles had moved to Athens in 454 BCE.

facts.quizQuestion: What dramatic innovation is Aeschylus credited with introducing to Greek theater, which Aristotle confirmed enabled true dramatic conflict for the first time?
facts.explanation: Before Aeschylus, Greek tragedy featured only one actor plus the chorus. By adding a second actor, Aeschylus created the possibility of two characters speaking to each other — making genuine dramatic conflict possible. Aristotle explicitly confirmed this in his Poetics. Sophocles later added a third actor, further expanding dramatic possibilities.
facts.statement: Aeschylus added the second actor to Greek drama, enabling true dramatic conflict — confirmed by Aristotle.
facts.wowFactor: Before Aeschylus, Greek 'drama' was basically one person telling a story to a singing choir. He invented the idea of two characters actually talking to each other — everything we recognize as dramatic storytelling flows from this single innovation.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two masked actors in theatrical robes facing each other dramatically on a circular stone stage, with a chorus visible behind them

variants.question: Which Greek playwright is credited with adding the third actor to drama, building on Aeschylus's earlier innovation?

facts.quizQuestion: Which 458 BCE work by Aeschylus is the only complete surviving tragic trilogy from ancient Greece, following the story of Agamemnon's murder and his son Orestes' revenge?
facts.explanation: The Oresteia (458 BCE) consists of three plays: Agamemnon (the king returns from Troy and is murdered by his wife Clytemnestra), The Libation Bearers (son Orestes takes revenge), and The Eumenides (Orestes is tried in Athens — the first jury trial in mythology). It is the only complete trilogy to survive from ancient Greece.
facts.statement: The Oresteia (458 BCE) by Aeschylus is the only complete surviving Greek tragic trilogy.
facts.wowFactor: The Oresteia ends with the invention of trial by jury — Orestes is acquitted by the goddess Athena casting the deciding vote. Aeschylus used mythology to celebrate Athens' legal system in front of a live Athenian audience.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art triptych of three scenes: a king returning in a chariot, a woman with a dagger, a tribunal of robed figures before an owl-helmeted goddess

variants.question: What is the name of the third and final play in Aeschylus's Oresteia trilogy, in which Orestes is put on trial?

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many plays did Sophocles write during his career, of which only 7 survive complete today?
facts.explanation: Sophocles wrote more than 120 plays over nearly 50 years of competing at Athenian dramatic festivals. He won 24 of his 30 contest entries and never placed below second — an extraordinary record. Of his 120+ works, only 7 survive complete, including Oedipus Rex, Antigone, and Oedipus at Colonus.
facts.statement: Sophocles wrote more than 120 plays; only 7 survive complete.
facts.wowFactor: Sophocles was so dominant that he never came in last place in any dramatic competition over nearly 50 years — winning 24 out of 30 contests. His rivals Aeschylus and Euripides each only won a fraction of that number.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an elder playwright with a laurel wreath writing on a scroll, surrounded by theatrical masks on a stone wall

variants.question: Which Greek tragic playwright won 24 of his 30 dramatic contest entries, never placing below second?

facts.quizQuestion: Which play by Sophocles, about a king who unknowingly kills his father and marries his mother, is widely considered the greatest of all ancient Greek tragedies?
facts.explanation: Oedipus Rex (also called Oedipus the King or Oedipus Tyrannus) is Sophocles' masterpiece and widely considered the pinnacle of Greek tragedy. Aristotle cited it as the model tragedy in his Poetics. The story follows King Oedipus as he investigates a plague-causing curse and discovers that he himself — unknowingly — killed his father and married his mother.
facts.statement: Oedipus Rex by Sophocles is widely considered the greatest ancient Greek tragedy.
facts.wowFactor: Aristotle held up Oedipus Rex as the perfect tragedy in his Poetics — written 100 years after the play premiered. Sigmund Freud named one of psychology's most famous concepts after it 2,400 years after that.

The conflict between Athens and Thebes

facts.quizQuestion: How many complete plays by Euripides survive today — more than Aeschylus and Sophocles combined?
facts.explanation: Euripides wrote 95+ plays and 19 survive complete — more than Aeschylus (7 surviving) and Sophocles (7 surviving) combined. Ironically, Euripides won only 5 dramatic competitions during his lifetime, far fewer than Sophocles' 24 wins. His works became enormously influential on Roman drama and the Renaissance revival of tragedy.
facts.statement: 19 plays by Euripides survive complete — more than Aeschylus and Sophocles combined.
facts.wowFactor: Euripides was considered controversial and won far fewer prizes than his rivals — ancient critics found his female characters too psychologically complex and his gods too fallible. Yet his 19 surviving plays outnumber all other tragedians. Popularity follows the controversial.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of three playwright busts on a shelf, the rightmost tallest, with scroll counts beneath each

variants.question: Which Greek tragedian's play Medea (431 BCE) features a sorceress who kills her own children to avenge her husband Jason's betrayal?

facts.quizQuestion: Which 411 BCE comedy by Aristophanes features women from across Greece withholding sex from their husbands until they agree to end the Peloponnesian War?
facts.explanation: Lysistrata (411 BCE) by Aristophanes is an antiwar comedy in which the Athenian woman Lysistrata organizes a sex strike by women of all Greek city-states to force the men to end the Peloponnesian War. Written during the war itself, the play is remarkable for its progressive portrayal of women with political agency — highly unusual in ancient Athens.
facts.statement: Aristophanes' Lysistrata (411 BCE) features women using a sex strike to end the Peloponnesian War.
facts.wowFactor: Aristophanes staged a play during an active war in which women of ENEMY city-states unite and force men to make peace — in front of an Athenian male audience that had been conscripted into that very war. Ancient comedy had nerve.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of determined women in colorful robes blocking the entrance to a temple treasury, gesturing firmly at embarrassed armored men

facts.quizQuestion: What title did the Roman orator Cicero bestow on the Greek writer Herodotus, who wrote the first comprehensive account of the Greco-Persian Wars?
facts.explanation: Cicero called Herodotus the 'Father of History' because his Histories represented the earliest Greek prose to survive intact and the first systematic attempt to investigate and record past events. Born c. 484 BCE in Halicarnassus (modern Turkey), Herodotus covered not just warfare but extensive cultural and geographical digressions about all the peoples involved.
facts.statement: Herodotus was called the 'Father of History' by Cicero for writing the earliest surviving comprehensive historical prose.
facts.wowFactor: Herodotus wrote about events from Egypt's pharaohs to Scythian burial customs to the sex habits of Babylonians — his Histories is essentially the world's first encyclopedia disguised as a war history. Ancient critics mocked him as a liar; modern archaeology keeps proving him right.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a bearded Greek scribe with a stylus writing on papyrus, surrounded by small illustrations of pyramids, ships, and warriors

variants.question: Which ancient writer is credited with writing the 'earliest Greek prose to have survived intact' — a comprehensive account of the Greco-Persian Wars?

facts.quizQuestion: What historical innovation is Thucydides credited with in his History of the Peloponnesian War — a method that excluded divine explanations and prioritized eyewitness testimony?
facts.explanation: Thucydides developed what scholars call 'scientific history' — strict standards of impartiality, reliance on eyewitness accounts, documentary evidence, and the deliberate exclusion of divine or supernatural explanations for events. This contrasted with Herodotus, who incorporated mythology and hearsay. Thucydides was himself a general in the war, exiled for 20 years after failing to prevent the Spartan capture of Amphipolis.
facts.statement: Thucydides invented scientific history — prioritizing impartiality and eyewitness evidence, excluding divine explanations.
facts.wowFactor: Thucydides was exiled for military failure, spent 20 years writing his masterpiece about the very war he was banished from — and his work still ended mid-sentence at 411 BCE, meaning he died before finishing it. The war ended in 404 BCE.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a lone exiled historian writing at a desk on a rocky clifftop, watching distant ships sail toward a burning city

variants.question: What event caused Thucydides to be exiled from Athens for 20 years during the Peloponnesian War?
variants.correctAnswer: Failing to prevent the Spartan capture of Amphipolis
distractors: Losing the Battle of Pylos against Sparta
Refusing to serve in the Sicilian Expedition
Writing criticism of Pericles' war strategy

facts.quizQuestion: What title is given to Hippocrates of Kos, born c. 460 BCE, who was the first person credited with believing diseases had natural rather than supernatural causes?
facts.explanation: Hippocrates, born c. 460 BCE on the island of Kos, separated medicine from religion and superstition for the first time. He developed clinical observation, prognosis, and systematic disease categorization. The Hippocratic Corpus (~60 texts attributed to his school) and the Hippocratic Oath both bear his name. His humoral theory (four humors: blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) dominated Western medicine for ~2,000 years.
facts.statement: Hippocrates, born c. 460 BCE on Kos, is called the 'Father of Medicine' for separating medicine from religion.
facts.wowFactor: Before Hippocrates, Greeks believed epilepsy was a sacred disease — divine punishment or possession. He was the first to insist it had a physical cause in the brain. It took the world another 2,400 years to figure out he was basically right.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Greek physician examining a patient on a stone bed, holding a clay tablet with notes, surrounded by herb bundles and clay jars

variants.question: What theory introduced by Hippocrates — involving blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile — dominated Western medicine for approximately 2,000 years?

facts.quizQuestion: Which colossal gold-and-ivory statue by sculptor Phidias, created around 432 BCE at a sanctuary in the western Peloponnese, was counted among the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World?
facts.explanation: Phidias created the Statue of Zeus at Olympia around 432 BCE — a colossal chryselephantine (gold and ivory) statue of the king of the gods seated on a throne in his sanctuary at Olympia. It was so awe-inspiring that ancient writers said one could not view it without feeling elevated. Like his Athena Parthenos in the Parthenon, it is now lost. Archaeologists found his workshop at Olympia with a cup inscribed 'I belong to Phidias.'
facts.statement: Phidias created the Statue of Zeus at Olympia (c. 432 BCE), one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
facts.wowFactor: Archaeologists excavating Olympia in 1954 found Phidias's actual workshop — and inside it, a small cup with the inscription 'I belong to Phidias' scratched into the clay. A 2,400-year-old signature.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a colossal golden enthroned god figure inside an ornate temple, tiny human figures standing in awe at the entrance

He fled to Olympia to escape Pericles' enemies

facts.quizQuestion: Aspasia — the long-term partner of Pericles and acclaimed rhetorician who hosted leading Athenian thinkers — was born in which Ionian city (modern Turkey) around 470 BCE?
facts.explanation: Aspasia was born c. 470 BCE in Miletus, an Ionian Greek city on the Aegean coast of modern Turkey. As a foreign-born woman (metic) in Athens, she could not hold Athenian citizenship. Ancient tradition portrayed her as a brilliant rhetorician and philosopher; Socrates reportedly visited her salon. She was satirized by Aristophanes and had at least one son with Pericles — Pericles the Younger.
facts.statement: Aspasia, partner of Pericles and celebrated rhetorician, was born in Miletus around 470 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Aspasia reportedly influenced some of the greatest minds in Athens — including Socrates, who said he learned rhetoric from her. Yet she was barred from Athenian citizenship simply for being born in the wrong city. She appears in five different ancient authors' works as a genuinely influential figure.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a graceful woman in fine robes at the center of a philosophical discussion circle, gesturing while surrounded by attentive Athenian men

variants.question: Why could Aspasia not hold Athenian citizenship despite living in Athens as Pericles' partner?
variants.correctAnswer: She was a foreign-born woman (metic) from Miletus
distractors: She was enslaved before Pericles freed her
Athenian law barred any unmarried woman from citizenship
She had previously been married to a Spartan officer
She refused to renounce her Ionian religious practices

facts.quizQuestion: Aeschylus — the father of Greek tragedy — reportedly chose to be remembered on his own epitaph not for his 70+ plays but for his participation in which famous battle of 490 BCE?
facts.explanation: Aeschylus fought at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BCE and at the Battle of Salamis in 480 BCE. According to ancient tradition, his self-composed epitaph mentioned only his military service at Marathon — not a single word about his plays. This reveals how highly Greeks valued military valor: even the man who invented tragic drama considered his greatest achievement fighting for Athens.
facts.statement: Aeschylus's epitaph, reportedly written by himself, mentioned his valor at Marathon — not his plays.
facts.wowFactor: The man who invented dramatic theater wanted to be remembered as a soldier, not a playwright. His self-written epitaph read something like: 'Beneath this stone lies Aeschylus the Athenian...the grove of Marathon can tell of his proven valor; the long-haired Mede knows it well.'
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Greek hoplite in full armor with a spear and shield, a scroll of plays visible but tucked beneath his arm

variants.question: Which playwright wrote The Persians (472 BCE) — the only surviving Greek tragedy based on a real historical event, the Battle of Salamis?

facts.quizQuestion: Which Greek tragedian employed the 'deus ex machina' device — a crane lowering actors playing gods to resolve plots — in more than half of his surviving plays, earning Aristotle's criticism for overusing it?
facts.explanation: The deus ex machina (god from the machine) used a mechanical crane (mechane) to lower actors playing gods onto the stage to resolve otherwise irresolvable plot situations. While Aeschylus introduced it, Euripides used it far more extensively — more than half his surviving tragedies employ it. Aristotle criticized this in his Poetics, arguing plots should resolve through internal logic, not divine intervention.
facts.statement: Euripides overused the deus ex machina device; Aristotle criticized him for it in the Poetics.
facts.wowFactor: The 'deus ex machina' literally means 'god from the machine' — the machine being a giant wooden crane that lifted actors playing gods over the stage. Ancient theater had its own special effects department. The phrase entered English to describe any implausible last-minute rescue in storytelling.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an actor in golden god costume dangling from ropes above a stone stage while the chorus below looks up in theatrical awe

variants.question: What is the theatrical term for the crane device that lowered actors playing gods onto the Greek stage to resolve plot complications?

facts.quizQuestion: In which 405 BCE Aristophanes comedy does the god Dionysus travel to the underworld to bring back Euripides, only to choose Aeschylus instead after a poetry competition?
facts.explanation: The Frogs (405 BCE) by Aristophanes is a comedy in which Dionysus (god of theater) descends to Hades because Athens has lost all its great tragedians — Euripides and Sophocles had both just died. In the underworld, Euripides and Aeschylus compete in a poetry contest; Dionysus ultimately chooses to bring back Aeschylus, whose older, more traditional style is judged superior. The play premiered just after Euripides' death.
facts.statement: In The Frogs (405 BCE), Dionysus descends to Hades and chooses Aeschylus over Euripides in a poetry contest.
facts.wowFactor: The Frogs premiered days after Euripides died — Aristophanes had been mocking him in plays for years, yet the comedy treated him as irreplaceable. When it won first prize, Athens gave it the rare honor of a second performance. Even in mockery, there was respect.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a portly god in lion skin and sandals rowing a small boat through a misty underworld river, with a frog chorus visible in the dark water

variants.question: What was the setting of the dramatic contest at the heart of Aristophanes' comedy The Frogs?
distractors: The Theater of Dionysus in Athens
The court of the Persian king

facts.quizQuestion: Herodotus, the 'Father of History,' was born around 484 BCE in which city on the Aegean coast of modern Turkey — which was then under Persian control?
facts.explanation: Herodotus was born c. 484 BCE in Halicarnassus (modern Bodrum, Turkey), a Greek city that was at the time under Persian control. This gave him a unique perspective — he grew up straddling the Greek and Persian worlds. He later traveled extensively, gathering accounts for his Histories from Egyptian priests, Scythian traders, and eyewitnesses across the ancient world.
facts.statement: Herodotus was born c. 484 BCE in Halicarnassus (modern Bodrum, Turkey), then under Persian control.
facts.wowFactor: Herodotus grew up in a Persian-controlled city, traveled to Egypt, Babylon, and the Black Sea to research his history, then helped Greeks understand the Persians — writing about both sides with unusual fairness for a 5th-century BCE author.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a traveler with a writing tablet standing at a crossroads sign, with Greek columns on one side and Persian arched gates on the other

variants.question: True or False: Herodotus was born in Athens and spent his entire life there writing the Histories.

facts.quizQuestion: What foundational medical text, attributed to Hippocrates, serves as the basis for similar oaths and laws defining good medical practice that physicians still swear versions of today?
facts.explanation: The Hippocratic Oath is an ancient oath traditionally attributed to Hippocrates and his school. It establishes ethical standards for medical practice — including doing no harm, maintaining patient confidentiality, and treating patients honestly. Modern medical graduates typically take updated versions. The Wikipedia article notes it 'serves as a foundation for other, similar oaths and laws that define good medical practice.'
facts.statement: The Hippocratic Oath, attributed to Hippocrates, remains the foundation for medical ethics oaths worldwide.
facts.wowFactor: The phrase 'first, do no harm' (primum non nocere) is widely attributed to the Hippocratic Oath — but it doesn't actually appear in the original oath. It's a Latin paraphrase of a concept from elsewhere in the Hippocratic Corpus. The most famous medical maxim is a misquote.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a physician raising his hand in a formal oath gesture before a scroll, with the caduceus symbol on the wall behind him

variants.question: What was Hippocrates' most important break from previous medical tradition?
variants.correctAnswer: He believed diseases had natural causes, not supernatural or divine causes
distractors: He invented the use of herbs and plant-based medicines

facts.quizQuestion: What dramatic innovation is Sophocles credited with introducing to Greek theater, building on Aeschylus's earlier addition of a second actor?
facts.explanation: Aeschylus had introduced the second actor; Sophocles took the next step by adding a third actor to Greek drama. He also reduced the narrative role of the chorus and is credited with introducing skenographia (scene-painting techniques). Having three actors dramatically expanded the range of scenes, relationships, and conflicts a playwright could stage simultaneously.
facts.statement: Sophocles introduced the third actor to Greek drama, and also pioneered scene-painting (skenographia).
facts.wowFactor: Greek tragedies used only three actors total for ALL the speaking roles — meaning one actor might play several different characters in the same play just by changing masks and costumes. Ancient theatergoers were watching what amounted to a one-actor show with costume changes.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of three masked actors in dramatically different robes — one royal, one warrior, one priest — standing in a triangular formation on a stage

variants.question: True or False: The three 'Theban plays' by Sophocles (Oedipus Rex, Antigone, Oedipus at Colonus) were written as a deliberate connected trilogy.

facts.quizQuestion: Which catastrophic event, which killed Pericles in 429 BCE and roughly 25% of Athens' population, did the historian Thucydides personally survive and describe in detail?
facts.explanation: The Plague of Athens (430–426 BCE) struck during the Peloponnesian War and killed approximately 75,000–100,000 people — roughly 25% of Athens' population, including Pericles in 429 BCE. Thucydides contracted the disease and survived, leaving us the most detailed ancient description of its symptoms. He recorded it with clinical precision, noting the moral collapse of Athenian society as people abandoned normal ethics.
facts.statement: Thucydides survived the Plague of Athens (430 BCE) and wrote the most detailed ancient description of it.
facts.wowFactor: Thucydides described how the plague caused social collapse — people stopped following laws because they figured they'd die anyway. Modern scientists studying his description have suggested it may have been typhoid fever, though the debate continues 2,400 years later.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a pale historian writing at a bedside, with sick figures on stone beds in the background and torch light flickering on disease-darkened walls

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the colossal gold-and-ivory statue by Phidias housed inside the Parthenon, dedicated c. 438 BCE and now known only from ancient copies?
facts.explanation: Phidias created the Athena Parthenos ('Athena the Virgin') — a chryselephantine (gold and ivory) cult statue approximately 12 meters tall inside the Parthenon, dedicated c. 438 BCE. The gold on the statue represented roughly 44 talents of gold (about 1,140 kg). Like all of Phidias's colossal works, it is now lost; we know it only through ancient written descriptions and later Roman copies.
facts.statement: The Athena Parthenos — a 12-meter gold-and-ivory statue by Phidias — stood inside the Parthenon, dedicated c. 438 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: The gold on the Athena Parthenos was removable — Pericles designed it that way so Athens could melt it down for war funds in an emergency. About 1,140 kg of gold literally stood inside the Parthenon as a strategic reserve for the city.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a colossal golden armored goddess filling the interior of a marble temple, her outstretched hand holding a small winged figure

variants.question: What material technique did Phidias specialize in for his most famous colossal statues — a combination of precious metals and organic material?

facts.quizQuestion: During which years did agents of Thomas Bruce (Lord Elgin) remove approximately half of the surviving Parthenon sculptures — now held at the British Museum and known as the Elgin Marbles?
facts.explanation: Between 1801 and 1812, agents working for Thomas Bruce, the 7th Earl of Elgin (then British Ambassador to the Ottoman Empire), removed approximately half of the surviving sculptural decorations from the Parthenon. The sculptures — now called the Elgin Marbles or Parthenon Marbles — were transported to Britain and have been displayed at the British Museum since 1832. Greece has repeatedly requested their return.
facts.statement: Agents of Lord Elgin removed approximately half the Parthenon sculptures between 1801 and 1812; they are now in the British Museum.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon sculptures sat undisturbed for 2,200 years — surviving Greek civilization, Roman rule, Byzantine conversion to a church, and Ottoman conversion to a mosque — before being hauled off to London in 19 years. The debate over returning them continues today.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of marble frieze panels being lowered by ropes from a temple wall onto carts, with British soldiers in tricorn hats supervising

facts.quizQuestion: Of approximately 40 comedies written by Aristophanes, how many survive virtually complete today — making them the most valuable surviving examples of Athenian Old Comedy?
facts.explanation: Aristophanes wrote approximately 40 plays, but only 11 survive virtually complete. These 11 plays — spanning from 425 BCE to 386 BCE — are the primary source of knowledge about Old Comedy, the politically sharp, bawdy, fantastical style of Athenian comedy from the 5th century BCE. His surviving works include The Acharnians, The Clouds, Lysistrata, The Birds, and The Frogs.
facts.statement: 11 of Aristophanes' ~40 comedies survive, making them the primary examples of Athenian Old Comedy.
facts.wowFactor: Aristophanes' comedies name and mock real Athenian politicians, generals, and intellectuals by name — they are essentially ancient political cartoons. Reading them is like watching a sharp 5th-century BCE satirical late night show that happened to survive 2,400 years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a comedian in an exaggerated padded costume and grotesque mask pointing accusingly at a scroll bearing a real Athenian politician's name

variants.question: Which Greek playwright is known as 'The Father of Comedy' and 'the Prince of Ancient Comedy'?

facts.quizQuestion: After divorcing his first wife around 445 BCE, Pericles formed a long-term intellectual and romantic partnership with which Milesian woman who became celebrated as a philosopher and rhetorician?
facts.explanation: Around 445 BCE, Pericles divorced his first wife and formed a lasting partnership with Aspasia of Miletus, a foreign-born woman (metic) known for her intelligence. Ancient sources describe her as a skilled rhetorician and philosopher who hosted Socrates and other leading Athenians in her salon. Though their exact relationship is debated (wife, concubine, or companion), she had at least one son — Pericles the Younger — with him.
facts.statement: Pericles formed a long-term intellectual and romantic partnership with Aspasia of Miletus after c. 445 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Aspasia was one of the most discussed women in ancient Athens — despite (or because of) being barred from citizenship. Socrates said he studied rhetoric with her. Plato named a dialogue after her. Aristophanes mocked her. She appears more in the historical record than almost any Athenian woman.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a man in a general's helmet and a woman in fine robes standing together on a rooftop terrace overlooking the Acropolis at sunset

variants.question: True or False: Aspasia held Athenian citizenship and was able to vote in the Athenian Assembly.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the title of Thucydides' major work — the foundational text of 'scientific history' that breaks off abruptly at 411 BCE, seven years before the war it describes actually ended?
facts.explanation: Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War is his only known work. Using strict standards of impartiality and eyewitness accounts, he chronicled the 27-year war between Athens and Sparta (431–404 BCE). His text breaks off mid-sentence at 411 BCE — suggesting he died before completing it. Later divided into 8 books by Alexandrian scholars. His influence extends to modern political realism.
facts.statement: Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War is the foundational text of 'scientific history,' unfinished at 411 BCE.
facts.wowFactor: Thucydides' book ends mid-sentence — not at a climactic moment but partway through a routine military description. He died without finishing it, 7 years before the war's end. We're still reading an incomplete draft from 2,400 years ago.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a scroll lying open, its final line trailing off incomplete, with a quill pen lying beside it in a pool of dried ink

variants.question: Thucydides owned gold mines in _____, which funded his historical research during his 20-year exile from Athens.

facts.quizQuestion: In which dialect of ancient Greek did Herodotus write his Histories — the same dialect used in the Ionian city where he was born?
facts.explanation: Herodotus wrote in the Ionic dialect of ancient Greek, which was the literary dialect of his birthplace Halicarnassus and the broader Ionian cultural sphere. This was different from the Attic dialect used by Athenian authors like Thucydides. The Ionic dialect and the Attic dialect were the two most prestigious literary dialects of classical Greek.
facts.statement: Herodotus wrote the Histories in the Ionic dialect of ancient Greek.
facts.wowFactor: Ancient Greek had multiple completely different dialects — Ionic, Doric, Attic, Aeolic — and literate Greeks could usually identify an author's origin just from their word choices. It was like the difference between Scottish, Cockney, and Southern American English, but more extreme.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an ancient papyrus scroll with Greek text visible, surrounded by small regional maps showing different Greek dialect zones

variants.question: True or False: Herodotus limited his Histories to military events, excluding cultural and geographical information about foreign peoples.

facts.quizQuestion: What killed Pericles in 429 BCE, ending the Golden Age leadership of Athens during the first years of the Peloponnesian War?
facts.explanation: Pericles died in 429 BCE from the Plague of Athens — the epidemic that struck in 430 BCE and killed roughly 25% of Athens' population over several years. The plague spread rapidly inside Athens partly because Pericles had ordered the population of Attica to shelter inside the city walls rather than defend rural areas against Spartan invasion. The crowded conditions accelerated the outbreak.
facts.statement: Pericles died in 429 BCE from the Plague of Athens, which killed roughly 25% of the city's population.
facts.wowFactor: The very military strategy that defined Pericles' genius — bringing everyone inside the walls to avoid Spartan land battles — created the overcrowding that killed him. His greatest tactical insight may have directly caused his death.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a once-proud general lying pale in a darkened room, his general's helmet set aside on a stone shelf, while a worried physician holds his wrist

variants.question: Pericles' military strategy of bringing all of Attica's population inside Athens' walls to avoid land battles with Sparta inadvertently contributed to which disaster?

The loss of the Battle of Aegospotami
The burning of the Agora by Spartan forces

facts.quizQuestion: Which 423 BCE Aristophanes comedy satirizes Socrates as a charlatan who runs a 'Thinkery' and teaches students to argue their way out of debt — which Plato believed contributed to Socrates' later execution?
facts.explanation: The Clouds (423 BCE) by Aristophanes depicts Socrates as a fraud running a 'Thinkery' (Phrontisterion), hanging in a basket to think about clouds, and teaching sophistic tricks for avoiding creditors. Plato, writing in the Apology, claimed the play helped create the hostile public image of Socrates that contributed to his trial and execution in 399 BCE — 24 years later.
facts.statement: Aristophanes' The Clouds (423 BCE) mocked Socrates; Plato credited it with contributing to Socrates' execution.
facts.wowFactor: Socrates himself reportedly attended the first performance of The Clouds and stood up so the audience could compare his face to the mask. He found it funny. He was accused of being exactly that man 24 years later and executed. A comedy may have literally helped kill history's most famous philosopher.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an old man hanging in a wicker basket suspended between pillars, staring at clouds and making notes on a tablet while students below scratch their heads

variants.question: What was the name of the satirical school where Aristophanes depicted Socrates teaching in The Clouds?

facts.quizQuestion: Which populist policy did Pericles introduce that allowed poor Athenian citizens to participate in the jury system without financial hardship — expanding democratic participation beyond the wealthy?
facts.explanation: Pericles introduced payment for jury service (and also public funding for theater attendance), allowing poorer citizens to take time off work to serve on juries and attend civic events. Before this reform, only those who could afford to forgo a day's wages could participate in the jury system. This was a significant expansion of practical democracy in Athens.
facts.statement: Pericles introduced paid jury service and public theater funding to enable poorer citizens to participate in Athenian democracy.
facts.wowFactor: Pericles essentially invented the democratic safety net — he knew democracy only works if everyone can actually afford to participate. Athens had jury fees, theater subsidies, and eventually pay for assembly attendance, all pioneered in his era.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a workman in rough clothes receiving a small bronze coin from a court official at the entrance to a columned courthouse

variants.question: What strategic approach did Pericles advocate for Athens in its conflict with Sparta during the Peloponnesian War?
variants.correctAnswer: Defensive naval power; avoiding direct land battles with Sparta
distractors: Aggressive land campaigns into the Peloponnese
Forming a military alliance with Persia against Sparta
Building a massive land army equal to Spartan forces
Using bribery and diplomacy to split the Spartan alliance

facts.quizQuestion: On which Greek island was Hippocrates, the 'Father of Medicine,' born around 460 BCE — an island famous for its Asclepion healing sanctuary and medical school?
facts.explanation: Hippocrates was born c. 460 BCE on Kos (also spelled Cos), a Greek island in the Aegean Sea near the coast of modern Turkey. Kos had a famous Asclepion — a healing sanctuary and medical school dedicated to the god Asclepius. It became the center of Greek medical practice. Hippocrates died c. 370 BCE in Larissa, aged approximately 83–90.
facts.statement: Hippocrates was born c. 460 BCE on the island of Kos, famous for its Asclepion medical sanctuary.
facts.wowFactor: The Asclepion on Kos was simultaneously a temple, a spa, and a medical school — patients would sleep overnight in the sanctuary, believe they received divine diagnosis in dreams, and then be treated by Hippocratic physicians in the morning. Religion and medicine shared the same institution.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Greek island harbor with a white-columned temple on a hilltop, patients visible walking up the sacred path below

variants.question: Hippocrates' school produced the Hippocratic Corpus — a collection of approximately _____ medical texts attributed to him and his followers.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the Delian League founded — the Athenian-led alliance of Greek city-states named for its original treasury location on a sacred Aegean island?
facts.explanation: The Delian League was founded in 478 BCE, immediately after the Greek victories over Persia at Salamis and Plataea. Named for the treasury kept on the sacred island of Delos, it was originally a genuine alliance of 150–330 Greek city-states to continue the war against Persia. Member states could contribute either ships/soldiers or money; most chose money, gradually making Athens dominant.
facts.statement: The Delian League was founded in 478 BCE as a Greek alliance against Persia, with its treasury on the island of Delos.
facts.wowFactor: The Delian League's treasury was on the 'neutral' sacred island of Delos because it belonged to Apollo — a god both sides respected. When Pericles moved it to Athens in 454 BCE, everyone understood it meant Athens now ran an empire, not an alliance.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Greek city-state envoys placing carved tribute tokens into a large bronze chest inside an island sanctuary, with an Apollo statue watching

variants.question: True or False: The Delian League was founded to fund construction of the Parthenon.

facts.quizQuestion: Which 431 BCE tragedy by Euripides features a sorceress who takes the most extreme possible revenge on her faithless husband Jason by killing their own children?
facts.explanation: Medea (431 BCE) by Euripides is one of the most psychologically complex Greek tragedies. Jason, hero of the Golden Fleece, abandons his foreign-born wife Medea to marry a Greek princess. In response, Medea kills the princess, the princess's father, and then her own two children to inflict maximum suffering on Jason. Euripides portrayed Medea with unprecedented psychological depth — she debates and agonizes before acting.
facts.statement: Euripides' Medea (431 BCE) depicts a sorceress who kills her own children to punish her faithless husband Jason.
facts.wowFactor: Earlier versions of the Medea myth had the children killed accidentally or by the Corinthians. Euripides was the first to make Medea herself kill them deliberately. Ancient audiences were reportedly shocked. Some scholars believe Euripides invented this element of the story.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a dark-robed woman standing alone on stone steps, a stormy sea visible behind her, the ashes of a ruined marriage around her feet

variants.question: The Bacchae (405 BCE) — performed posthumously after its author's death — is a tragedy about what violent mythological event?
variants.correctAnswer: The god Dionysus driving Theban women mad to take revenge on King Pentheus who denied his divinity
distractors: Achilles' rage following the death of Patroclus
Agamemnon's murder by his wife Clytemnestra
Orestes being pursued by the Furies

facts.quizQuestion: The Parthenon is a temple built in which Greek architectural order — characterized by its sturdy, plain columns without a base and simple, undecorated capitals?
facts.explanation: The Parthenon is a Doric temple — the oldest and simplest of the three main Greek architectural orders (Doric, Ionic, Corinthian). Doric columns have no base, sit directly on the floor (stylobate), and have simple circular capitals. The Parthenon's exterior uses the Doric order while incorporating some Ionic elements (like the continuous Ionic frieze that ran around the interior of the colonnade).
facts.statement: The Parthenon is built in the Doric order — the simplest of the three main Greek architectural styles.
facts.wowFactor: The Parthenon is technically not a perfect rectangle — the floor curves upward in the center by about 6 cm, the columns lean slightly inward, and the corner columns are fatter than the others. All deliberate optical corrections to make a building that looks perfectly straight when it isn't.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art showing three column styles side by side labeled Doric (plain), Ionic (scroll), and Corinthian (leafy), with the Parthenon silhouette using the first

variants.question: Which Greek architectural order features columns with scroll-shaped (volute) capitals and is associated with the more elegant Ionian style?

facts.quizQuestion: What did Thales of Miletus — considered the first philosopher in the Western tradition — propose as the single fundamental substance (arche) underlying all of existence?
facts.explanation: Thales of Miletus (c. 626–548 BCE) proposed that water is the arche — the fundamental substance from which all things are made. This was revolutionary not because the answer was correct, but because he was the first to seek a rational, natural explanation instead of a mythological one. Aristotle is the primary source for this claim. Thales was also one of the Seven Sages of Greece.
facts.statement: Thales of Miletus proposed water as the fundamental substance underlying all existence — the first philosopher to seek a rational explanation for the cosmos.
facts.wowFactor: Thales was spectacularly wrong about water, but he changed the world by asking the question at all. Before him, everything was explained by gods fighting each other. After him, every scientist who ever asked 'what is everything made of?' was following his method.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an elderly Greek sage kneeling by a stream, looking at his own reflection with deep contemplation while a starry sky shows above

variants.question: What made Thales' proposal about water philosophically significant, even though it was scientifically wrong?
variants.correctAnswer: He was the first to give a rational natural explanation rather than a mythological one
distractors: He correctly predicted that water would be discovered to contain atoms
He proved water was necessary for life through controlled experiments
He was the first philosopher to write down his ideas in a published treatise
He convinced all other Greek thinkers to abandon religion immediately

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Thales of Miletus famously predict a solar eclipse — a feat that reportedly stopped a battle between the Lydians and Medes when the sky suddenly went dark?
facts.explanation: Thales predicted a solar eclipse that occurred in 585 BCE. According to Herodotus, the eclipse occurred during a battle between the Lydians and Medes, and both sides interpreted the sudden darkness as a divine sign to stop fighting and make peace. Modern astronomers have confirmed that a total solar eclipse was visible in that region around that time, validating the date.
facts.statement: Thales predicted the solar eclipse of 585 BCE — reportedly stopping a battle when it darkened the sky.
facts.wowFactor: A solar eclipse literally ended a war in 585 BCE, and an ancient Greek philosopher had called it in advance. Modern astronomers confirmed the eclipse really happened on May 28, 585 BCE. It's the earliest confirmed dated event in Greek history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two armies frozen mid-clash as a black disc covers the sun and soldiers from both sides stare upward in terror

variants.question: Thales of Miletus is considered the first philosopher in which major Western intellectual tradition?

facts.quizQuestion: What term did Anaximander use for the infinite, boundless, undefined source from which all things emerge — his alternative to Thales' theory that water was the fundamental substance?
facts.explanation: Anaximander (c. 610–546 BCE), successor to Thales in the Milesian school, rejected the idea that any specific element (water, fire, etc.) could be the fundamental substance. Instead, he proposed the apeiron (ἄπειρον) — the 'boundless' or 'indefinite' — as the source of all things. His reasoning was that no observable element could encompass all natural opposites; the source must itself be beyond all specific qualities.
facts.statement: Anaximander proposed the apeiron (the boundless/indefinite) as the source of all existence, rejecting single-element theories.
facts.wowFactor: Anaximander's 'boundless indefinite' is arguably closer to modern physics' concept of quantum fields or the primordial singularity than anything else in ancient thought. He got there by pure logic 2,600 years ago: any specific thing can't explain everything, so the source must be beyond all specific things.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a philosopher gesturing at an infinite void filled with swirling opposites — fire and ice, light and dark — merging into a featureless grey

variants.question: What practical cartographic contribution is Anaximander credited with, making him a pioneer in geography?
variants.correctAnswer: Creating one of the first known world maps
distractors: Inventing the compass rose for navigation
Measuring the circumference of the Earth
Drawing the first map of the Mediterranean Sea routes

acceptableAlternatives: you cannot step into the same river twice
distractors: Man is the measure of all things
facts.quizQuestion: Which doctrine is Heraclitus most famous for — often summarized as the idea that constant change and flux are the fundamental nature of reality, associated with the river metaphor?
facts.explanation: Heraclitus of Ephesus (c. 500 BCE) developed the doctrine of flux — the idea that everything is constantly changing and that change itself is the fundamental nature of reality. This is associated with the saying 'you cannot step into the same river twice' and the phrase panta rhei ('everything flows'), though scholars debate whether Heraclitus used that exact phrase. He also identified fire as the primary element and introduced the concept of the Logos (rational principle).
facts.statement: Heraclitus is most famous for the doctrine of flux: constant change as the fundamental nature of reality, the 'you cannot step into the same river twice' philosopher.
facts.wowFactor: Heraclitus was known in antiquity as 'the Obscure' or 'the Riddler' because he wrote in dense paradoxes. His collected sayings were reportedly so hard to understand that Socrates said you needed to be a deep-sea diver to retrieve their meaning.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a philosopher crouching beside a flowing river, one hand in the water, contemplating the constant motion while a crackling fire burns behind him

variants.question: What did Heraclitus identify as the primary material element, linking it to the Logos (rational principle governing the universe)?

facts.quizQuestion: Parmenides of Elea is considered the founder of which branch of philosophy — the study of being itself, asking questions like 'What exists?' and 'What does it mean to be?'
facts.explanation: Parmenides (c. 515–450s BCE), born in Elea in southern Italy, is considered the founder of ontology — the study of being. His core argument was that only Being exists — it is eternal, unchanging, indivisible. Non-being cannot exist. Therefore, change, generation, and destruction are illusions (they would require non-being to exist). This radical position influenced Plato's Theory of Forms and challenged atomists like Democritus.
facts.statement: Parmenides is considered the founder of ontology — he argued only Being exists, and change is an illusion.
facts.wowFactor: Parmenides argued logically that nothing can change, nothing can begin, and nothing can end — because all of these require 'non-being' to exist, and non-being cannot exist. It's completely wrong, but the argument is airtight. It took Aristotle 100 years to figure out how to refute it properly.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stern philosopher carving 'BEING IS' into a stone tablet while dynamically changing landscapes (seasons, fire, water) appear as ghost images behind him

variants.question: What two 'paths' did Parmenides describe in his philosophical poem 'On Nature'?
variants.correctAnswer: The Way of Truth (rational knowledge of Being) and the Way of Opinion (unreliable sensory perception)
distractors: The Way of Virtue (moral excellence) and the Way of Vice (corruption)
The Way of Form (ideal reality) and the Way of Matter (physical reality)
The Way of the Logos (reason) and the Way of Mythos (tradition)
The Way of the Gods (divine knowledge) and the Way of Mortals (limited knowledge)

facts.quizQuestion: Which mathematical theorem states that in a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides — and is named after the philosopher who is credited with its formal proof in the Greek tradition?
facts.explanation: The Pythagorean Theorem is attributed to Pythagoras of Samos (c. 570–495 BCE) in the Greek tradition, though Babylonian and Indian mathematicians knew the principle centuries earlier. Pythagoras is credited with its formal geometric proof. His broader philosophy held that mathematical principles underlie all of reality: 'its principles are the principles of everything.'
facts.statement: The Pythagorean Theorem (a²+b²=c²) is named for Pythagoras, though Babylonians knew the principle earlier; he is credited with its formal proof.
facts.wowFactor: Babylonian clay tablets from 1,000 years before Pythagoras show they already knew his theorem. What Pythagoras actually contributed was the idea that this mathematical relationship is a universal truth about reality — turning a practical formula into a philosophical statement about the nature of the universe.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a philosopher gesturing at a stone-carved right triangle with squares on each side, students crowded around him on a hillside in sunlight

variants.question: What strict communal organization did Pythagoras establish in Croton, southern Italy around 530 BCE?
variants.correctAnswer: The Pythagorean Brotherhood — a secretive community with shared possessions, vows, and dietary restrictions
distractors: The Milesian Academy — a public school open to all male citizens
The Delphic Guild — a group of oracle interpreters
The Sophistic Circle — paid teachers of rhetoric in public spaces
The Eleatic School — philosophers who debated in the Agora

facts.quizQuestion: In which city in southern Italy did Pythagoras found his secretive philosophical brotherhood around 530 BCE — a community whose members shared possessions and reportedly avoided eating beans?
facts.explanation: Around 530 BCE, Pythagoras settled in Croton (Kroton) in southern Italy (Magna Graecia) after leaving his birthplace of Samos. There he founded a secretive religious-philosophical community — the Pythagorean Brotherhood — whose members shared possessions, took vows of silence, followed strict dietary rules (including the famous prohibition on beans), and practiced mathematics as a sacred activity.
facts.statement: Pythagoras founded his Brotherhood in Croton, southern Italy, around 530 BCE — a community that reportedly avoided eating beans.
facts.wowFactor: The Pythagorean bean taboo was real and famous in antiquity. Ancient explanations ranged from 'beans cause flatulence which disturbs contemplation' to 'beans contain the souls of the dead.' Pythagoras himself may have been killed when he refused to cross a bean field to escape pursuers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of robed community members sharing a meal on long benches, conspicuously avoiding a plate of beans pushed to one side of the table

variants.question: Pythagoras' 'music of the spheres' theory held that planets and stars produce what, based on their mathematical orbital relationships?
variants.correctAnswer: A celestial harmony inaudible to humans
distractors: Random chaotic noise only mathematicians can decode
Perfect silence due to the mathematical precision of their orbits
A harmonic sound only heard at death
A drone note that underlies all earthly music

facts.quizQuestion: What did Democritus propose as the two fundamental realities underlying all of existence — the smallest indivisible units and the empty space between them?
facts.explanation: Democritus (c. 460–370 BCE), working with Leucippus, developed atomic theory: all matter consists of atoms — the smallest, indivisible particles — moving eternally through void (empty space). Atoms vary in size and shape; observable qualities like color and temperature are not intrinsic to matter but arise from how atoms interact. Only atoms and void are real. This was a joint theory with Leucippus, though later sources often attribute it solely to Democritus.
facts.statement: Democritus proposed atomic theory: all matter consists of indivisible atoms moving through void — the only true realities.
facts.wowFactor: Democritus proposed atoms in the 5th century BCE without any experimental equipment — purely through philosophical reasoning. He was spectacularly right in the big picture. The main thing he got wrong was that atoms are divisible (protons, neutrons, electrons). He still basically described chemistry 2,400 years before it existed.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a grinning philosopher holding up two fingers, one with a tiny glowing dot (atom) and one with empty space between them (void), both hovering above his palm

variants.question: Why was Democritus called 'the Laughing Philosopher' in antiquity?
variants.correctAnswer: He had a cheerful, amused view of life and found human folly funny rather than tragic
distractors: He made jokes in his philosophical writings to make them easier to read
He laughed when his atomic theory was rejected by Plato and Aristotle
He taught his students to laugh as a form of intellectual detachment
He believed laughter was the highest human good, superior even to wisdom

acceptableAlternatives: four elements: earth water fire air
facts.quizQuestion: Which pre-Socratic philosopher from Akragas, Sicily proposed that all matter consists of four fundamental 'roots' — earth, water, fire, and air — a theory that dominated Western science for two thousand years?
facts.explanation: Empedocles (c. 494–434 BCE) of Akragas (Sicily) proposed that all matter is composed of four fundamental elements (which he called 'roots'): earth, water, fire, and air. He also associated them with gods: Zeus (fire), Hera (air), Nestis (water), Aidoneus/Hades (earth). This four-element theory became the dominant scientific framework for ~2,000 years and was reinforced by Aristotle.
facts.statement: Empedocles proposed the four-element theory (earth, water, fire, air) — standard scientific dogma for 2,000 years.
facts.wowFactor: Empedocles also proposed that two cosmic forces — Love (attraction between different things) and Strife (separation) — drive all change in the universe. He basically invented a physics based on emotional forces. It's completely wrong, but it's oddly beautiful.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a philosopher gesturing at four stone tablets labeled with ancient Greek symbols, each glowing with its respective element color: brown, blue, red, white

variants.question: According to Empedocles, what two cosmic forces drive all change by causing attraction and separation between the four elements?

facts.quizQuestion: What systematic questioning technique is Socrates credited with developing — a method of probing beliefs through dialogue to expose contradictions and arrive at deeper understanding?
facts.explanation: The Socratic method (also called elenchus) is a form of cooperative argumentative dialogue based on asking and answering questions to stimulate critical thinking and expose the limits of claimed knowledge. Socrates used it to examine ethical and philosophical beliefs, typically showing that his interlocutor's confident claims were actually self-contradictory. It remains the foundation of modern legal cross-examination and philosophical education.
facts.statement: Socrates developed the Socratic method (elenchus) — a questioning technique to expose contradictions and examine beliefs through dialogue.
facts.wowFactor: Socrates famously claimed he knew nothing — and then would talk to Athens' most confident experts and prove via questions alone that they didn't know anything either. His technique of 'I'm just asking questions' made him simultaneously famous and hated enough to be executed.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a stocky barefoot philosopher in simple robes gesturing questioningly at a richly robed Athenian who looks confused and increasingly frustrated

variants.question: True or False: Socrates wrote extensive philosophical texts that are the primary source for his ideas.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Socrates tried before a jury of approximately 500 Athenians, found guilty of impiety and corrupting the youth, and executed by drinking hemlock?
facts.explanation: Socrates was tried and executed in 399 BCE. He was charged with asebeia (impiety) and corrupting Athenian youth. Tried before a jury of ~500 citizens, he was found guilty. When asked to propose his own punishment, he suggested free meals from the state — the jury rejected this and sentenced him to death. He refused to escape (his wealthy friend Crito had bribed the guards) and drank hemlock.
facts.statement: Socrates was tried and executed in 399 BCE, convicted of impiety and corrupting the youth by ~500 Athenian jurors.
facts.wowFactor: Socrates was offered a way out — his friend Crito had bribed the prison guards and had escape arranged — but he refused. He argued that escaping would contradict his lifelong position that citizens must obey the law, even unjust laws. He chose death rather than philosophical inconsistency.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a serene elderly philosopher sitting calmly on a stone bed holding a cup of hemlock, surrounded by weeping students, in a torchlit prison cell

variants.question: What punishment did Socrates propose for himself at his trial — which so infuriated the jury they voted for the death penalty?
variants.correctAnswer: Free meals from the state (at the Prytaneion)
distractors: A modest fine of one mina of silver
Five years of unpaid military service

facts.quizQuestion: Around what year did Plato found the Academy in Athens — considered the first institution of higher learning in the Western world, which operated for approximately 900 years?
facts.explanation: Plato founded the Academy around 387 BCE near a sacred grove of Hecademus outside Athens. It is considered the first institution of higher learning in the Western world. Aristotle studied there for ~20 years. The Academy remained active until Emperor Justinian closed it in 529 CE — operating for roughly 900 years. Plato founded it after returning from travels following Socrates' execution in 399 BCE.
facts.statement: Plato founded the Academy in Athens c. 387 BCE — the first Western institution of higher learning, operating ~900 years.
facts.wowFactor: The Academy Plato founded in 387 BCE outlasted the Roman Empire and wasn't closed until 529 CE — by the Christian emperor Justinian, who banned pagan philosophy schools. It operated for 916 years. Harvard has been around for 388 years so far.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of students in robes walking and debating in a shaded grove with stone benches, an olive tree at the center and Athens' walls visible in the background

variants.question: Plato's Republic describes the ideal state as being governed by whom?
distractors: A council of priests who interpret divine will
Wealthy merchant-citizens who fund public works

facts.quizQuestion: What famous thought experiment in Plato's Republic depicts prisoners chained in a cave who mistake shadows on a wall for reality — used as a metaphor for philosophical enlightenment?
facts.explanation: In the Allegory of the Cave (Republic, Book VII), Plato describes prisoners chained in a cave who can only see shadows cast on a wall — which they take to be reality. A philosopher is like a prisoner who breaks free, sees actual objects in sunlight, and eventually looks at the sun itself (representing the Form of the Good). The allegory illustrates Plato's distinction between the world of shadows (sensory perception) and the world of Forms (true reality).
facts.statement: Plato's Allegory of the Cave (Republic) depicts prisoners mistaking shadows for reality — a metaphor for philosophical enlightenment.
facts.wowFactor: Plato wrote the Allegory of the Cave 2,400 years ago, and it still shows up in The Matrix, Westworld, and virtually every 'is reality real?' story in modern entertainment. The shadow-prisoners who resist the freed philosopher and want to kill him? Plato's nod to the Athenians who executed Socrates.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of chained figures in silhouette facing a stone wall covered in dancing shadows, with a bright tunnel exit glowing behind them that one freed person is walking toward

variants.question: Plato's Theory of Forms holds that the material world we perceive is an imperfect copy of what?
variants.correctAnswer: An unchanging realm of abstract ideal Forms, apprehended by reason alone
distractors: A divine blueprint kept on Mount Olympus
A mathematical reality of perfect geometric solids
